title: On Optimal Interpolation in Linear Regression

reviews.summary: This paper is concerned with inducing a certain type of best possible interpolator in linear regression. It is based on the notion of best possible interpolator, defined in Muthukumar et al., 2019., which is the best estimator (i.e., with minimum risk) among the class of interpolators. Based on that, the authors define a new theoretical estimator, the best estimator among interpolators that are also linear in the response variable.  They manage to induce a closed form of this estimator and prove that it is the limit of preconditional gradient descent under a specific initialization. In addition, using approximations for quantities that are usually unknown in practice (e.g.  population covariance matrix, signal to noise ratio), they turn this theoretical estimator into a fully empirical one and compare its performance to other frequently used estimators, showing that this estimator performs arbitrarily better in certain cases. Finally, they extend this notion of best possible theoretical estimator to the random features setting, where they again induce a closed form for this estimator and prove that it is the limit of preconditioned gradient descent under a specific initialization.
reviews.main_review:
The paper is interesting to read. It belongs to the long list of papers that are concerned with the overparametrized estimation, but instead of trying to explain why this type of estimation generalizes so well, it aims to find the best linear interpolator in the linear regression setting. By restricting the class of possible estimators in the definition by Muthukumar et al., 2019, they manage to turn a theoretical device into a fully observable estimator. They also define a similar notion of optimal estimator for a more complex model and induce a closed form of this estimator, something that may suggests that we could try to induce this type of optimal estimators for even more complex model like neural networks. There is also an interesting example, where we can see that a frequently used interpolator, the minimum-norm interpolator, has arbitrarily bad generalization. Finally, these examples combined with the results related to preconditional gradient descent highlight nicely the importance of initialization.

The paper could be of greater significance if
a) the construction of the fully-empirical estimator would not be restricted to the isotropic regime
b) (as they note) it included a precise guarantee on the approximation error of the estimation of the covariance matrix Σ
c) instead of performing an empirical analysis for special cases, they could manage to prove a theoretical result that bounds the generalization error of the fully-empirical estimator w.r.t. the generalization error of the theoretical optimal response-linear achievable interpolator.

In addition, I think that some points in the beginning of the proof of Proposition 1 could be written with more clarity.

- 132 "In linear regression (when d>n), minimizing the..."
-480  Maybe you could start with a general W that belongs in this class instead of Wo, as equation (17) seems a bit strange.

reviews.text:
# Peer Review - Rewritten

## Summary

This work addresses the problem of constructing an optimal interpolator for linear regression by building upon the framework introduced in Muthukumar et al., 2019. The authors introduce a refined notion of optimality—specifically, the best estimator within the restricted class of response-linear interpolators—and derive its closed-form solution. A key theoretical contribution is establishing that this optimal estimator emerges as the limiting behavior of preconditioned gradient descent under appropriate initialization conditions. The authors then develop a practical version of this theoretical estimator by substituting empirical approximations for unknown population quantities (such as the covariance structure and signal-to-noise ratio). The methodology is further extended to the random features regime, where analogous closed-form solutions and convergence guarantees are established.

## Main Assessment

The paper presents compelling and well-motivated research that fits naturally within the broader literature on overparametrized regression. Rather than focusing on generalization phenomena in overparametrized settings, the authors take a different approach by identifying the optimal response-linear interpolator. By imposing this linearity constraint on the framework of Muthukumar et al., 2019, they successfully transform an abstract theoretical concept into a practical, observable estimator. The extension to random features demonstrates the potential generality of their approach and suggests promising directions for future work on more complex model classes, including neural networks. The paper also includes illuminating examples demonstrating that commonly-used interpolators like the minimum-norm solution can exhibit arbitrarily poor generalization performance. Together with the findings on preconditioned gradient descent, these results effectively underscore the critical role that initialization plays in determining estimator quality.

## Areas for Improvement

The paper would achieve greater impact if the following issues were addressed:

a) The construction of the practical empirical estimator is currently limited to the isotropic setting; relaxing this restriction would broaden the applicability of the results.

b) As the authors acknowledge, the paper lacks formal guarantees quantifying the approximation error incurred when estimating the population covariance matrix Σ. Providing such bounds would strengthen the theoretical foundations.

c) Rather than relying solely on empirical investigations of specific cases, the authors should endeavor to establish a theoretical result that bounds the generalization error of the fully-empirical estimator relative to the theoretical optimal response-linear interpolator. This would provide stronger guarantees on practical performance.

Additionally, certain portions of the proof of Proposition 1, particularly in the early stages, would benefit from more careful exposition and clearer presentation.

## Technical Comments and Minor Issues

- **Line 64**: The citation to Mourtada appears questionable, as that work does not appear to address interpolators.

- **Line 129**: A clarifying remark about the subscript notation in expectations would be helpful, since this notation can ambiguously refer to either conditional expectation given a particular quantity or expectation over all variables except that quantity.

- **Line 132**: The statement "In linear regression (when d>n), minimizing the..." would benefit from more precise formulation.

- **Line 480**: The presentation would be improved by beginning with a general matrix W from the specified class rather than immediately introducing W₀, as equation (17) appears somewhat unmotivated in its current form.

## Recommendation

**Rating: 6 (Marginally above the acceptance threshold)**

The paper makes solid theoretical contributions to understanding optimal interpolators in linear regression and demonstrates interesting connections to gradient descent dynamics. However, the limitations outlined above—particularly the restriction to isotropic settings, the absence of approximation error guarantees, and the lack of theoretical performance bounds for the empirical estimator—prevent this from being a strong accept. The work is sufficiently novel and technically sound to warrant publication, but addressing these concerns would substantially enhance its significance and practical utility.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper is concerned with inducing a certain type of best possible interpolator in linear regression. It is based on the notion of best possible interpolator, defined in Muthukumar et al., 2019., which is the best estimator (i.e., with minimum risk) among the class of interpolators. Based on that, the authors define a new theoretical estimator, the best estimator among interpolators that are also linear in the response variable.  They manage to induce a closed form of this estimator and prove that it is the limit of preconditional gradient descent under a specific initialization. In addition, using approximations for quantities that are usually unknown in practice (e.g.  population covariance matrix, signal to noise ratio), they turn this theoretical estimator into a fully empirical one and compare its performance to other frequently used estimators, showing that this estimator performs arbitrarily better in certain cases. Finally, they extend this notion of best possible theoretical estimator to the random features setting, where they again induce a closed form for this estimator and prove that it is the limit of preconditioned gradient descent under a specific initialization.

Main Review:
The paper is interesting to read. It belongs to the long list of papers that are concerned with the overparametrized estimation, but instead of trying to explain why this type of estimation generalizes so well, it aims to find the best linear interpolator in the linear regression setting. By restricting the class of possible estimators in the definition by Muthukumar et al., 2019, they manage to turn a theoretical device into a fully observable estimator. They also define a similar notion of optimal estimator for a more complex model and induce a closed form of this estimator, something that may suggests that we could try to induce this type of optimal estimators for even more complex model like neural networks. There is also an interesting example, where we can see that a frequently used interpolator, the minimum-norm interpolator, has arbitrarily bad generalization. Finally, these examples combined with the results related to preconditional gradient descent highlight nicely the importance of initialization.

The paper could be of greater significance if
a) the construction of the fully-empirical estimator would not be restricted to the isotropic regime
b) (as they note) it included a precise guarantee on the approximation error of the estimation of the covariance matrix Σ
c) instead of performing an empirical analysis for special cases, they could manage to prove a theoretical result that bounds the generalization error of the fully-empirical estimator w.r.t. the generalization error of the theoretical optimal response-linear achievable interpolator.

In addition, I think that some points in the beginning of the proof of Proposition 1 could be written with more clarity.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
In line:
-64  I don't think that Mourtada deals with interpolators in this paper.
-129 A comment regarding the subscript in the expectation may add some more clarity, as some times this means expected value given this quantity, or expected value given everything else other than this quantity
- 132 "In linear regression (when d>n), minimizing the..."
-480  Maybe you could start with a general W that belongs in this class instead of Wo, as equation (17) seems a bit strange.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper studies the generalization error of a class of interpolator in linear regression and identifies conditions for optimality. They consider a setting where there is a true linear model generating the data, whose parameters are drawn from a Gaussian distribution with zero-mean and covariance Phi. In this setting they consider a natural class of estimators that are a function of the data, the population covariance matrix, the prior covariance matrix Phi and the signal to noise ratio. Among this class of estimators they calculate a closed-form expression for the optimal interpolator, with respect to the expected excess risk and show that there are certain settings where the expected excess risk of this estimator can be much lower than the risk of the OLS solution. They also provide an extension of their results to the random features model.
reviews.main_review:
The authors study an interesting problem on a topic of much recent interest---benign overfitting of linear models. While the assumptions made on the problem are pretty stringent, the authors provide a clean exposition of their results which I appreciated as a reader. 

Here are some questions/comments about the paper:

1. The authors mention the results by Amari et al. (2021), who they mentioned studied estimators whose asymptotic bias or variance is optimal. How do the techniques and setting used in this paper differ from theirs? Can the techniques used there also be used here but instead to balance the bias and the variance optimally? A discussion regarding this would be helpful to the reader. 

2. On ln 91 the authors say an interesting question that they study here is if minimum norm interpolant is always close to optimal, and show that this is not the case. But I think this question has already partially been answered given past work of Bartlett et al. (2020). Given their results, it is easy to construct cases, for example, when population covariance matrix is isotropic, where the minimum norm interpolant is not consistent but the ridge regression solution is. So, I would urge the authors to provide some more nuanced context and discussion here.

3. Regarding the assumptions both the noise xi and the true parameter w* are assumed to be drawn from a Gaussian distribution. Can this be relaxed? Further, can the results of this paper (Proposition 1 in particular) be extended to hold in the case where w* is not mean zero?

4. In the experiments described in Section 4.1, where w_O is approximated by w_{O_e}, what is the structure of the population covariance matrix? Is it isotropic or close to it? Or does it have a heavy tailed structure similar to one that was shown to be necessary by Bartlett et al. for the minimum norm solution to be consistent? Further, empirically, was the estimate Sigma_e close to Sigma? A plot to verify this would be nice.


=================== Post rebuttal comments =================== 

Firstly, I would like to thank the authors for their very carefully response to the questions raised by all of the reviewers. I continue to support acceptance of this paper post the rebuttal period. In my view, I think it would be nice if the authors incorporate the many helpful suggestions raised in all of the reviews in a subsequent version.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: The authors do a good job of describing the potential negative impact of their work.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

reviews.text:
# Peer Review - Rewritten

## Summary

This work investigates the generalization error bounds for a specific class of interpolators within the linear regression framework and establishes conditions under which these estimators achieve optimality. The authors consider a setting where data is generated from a true linear model with parameters distributed according to a zero-mean Gaussian with covariance matrix Phi. They examine a natural family of estimators constructed from the observed data, the empirical covariance, the prior covariance Phi, and the noise-to-signal ratio. The paper derives a closed-form solution for the optimal interpolator in this class with respect to expected excess risk, demonstrating scenarios where this estimator substantially outperforms ordinary least squares. The authors additionally extend their framework to encompass the random features setting.

## Main Review

The paper addresses a compelling research question within the contemporary literature on benign overfitting in linear models. While the problem setup imposes certain restrictive assumptions, I found the authors' presentation to be clear and well-structured, which enhances readability.

I offer the following observations and questions:

1. The authors reference prior work by Amari et al. (2021) concerning estimators with asymptotically optimal bias or variance properties. It would strengthen the paper to elaborate on how the technical approach and problem formulation here diverge from that work. Specifically, could the techniques from Amari et al. be adapted to simultaneously optimize the bias-variance tradeoff in this context? The paper would benefit from a more thorough comparison of these methodological frameworks.

2. At line 91, the authors pose the question of whether the minimum norm interpolant consistently achieves near-optimal performance, and they demonstrate that this is not universally true. However, this question has been partially addressed in prior literature, particularly by Bartlett et al. (2020). Their results readily enable construction of counterexamples—for instance, when the population covariance is isotropic, the minimum norm interpolant fails to be consistent while ridge regression succeeds. I would recommend that the authors provide more sophisticated contextualization and discussion of their contribution relative to these existing findings.

3. The analysis assumes both the noise term ξ and the true parameter vector w* follow Gaussian distributions. Are these assumptions necessary, or could they be relaxed? Additionally, can the main results, particularly Proposition 1, be generalized to accommodate non-zero-mean distributions for w*?

4. In the experimental section (Section 4.1), where w_O is approximated by w_{O_e}, what characterizes the structure of the population covariance matrix? Is it approximately isotropic, or does it exhibit the heavy-tailed structure that Bartlett et al. identified as necessary for consistency of the minimum norm solution? Moreover, how closely does the empirical estimate Σ_e approximate the true Σ? Including a visualization of this comparison would be valuable.

## Post-Rebuttal Assessment

I appreciate the authors' thorough and thoughtful responses to the questions posed during the review process. Following the rebuttal, I maintain my recommendation for acceptance. The paper would be further strengthened by incorporating the constructive suggestions raised across all reviews in a future revision.

## Limitations and Societal Impact

The authors provide an adequate discussion of potential negative implications of their work.

## Recommendation

**Rating: 7 – Good paper, accept**

This paper makes solid contributions to an important topic with clear exposition. While operating under certain restrictive assumptions, the work provides valuable insights into the behavior of interpolating estimators. Acceptance is recommended.

reviews.summary: The paper under reviews studies the problem of optimal linear interpolation. More precisely the authors derive an explicit formula for the *best* linear interpolator that can be written as a linear function of the outputs and as a function of desired quantities. From this, they show how to recover theoretically this estimator from a optimization algorithm. Finally they expose cases where the designed estimator show some good generalization properties while the miminum norm interpolator does not.  The link with kernel methods is also made.
reviews.main_review:
**Overall comments and impression**

First, I would like to say that I am not an expert in statistical learning theory, so that I may have missed some insights in the results.

To begin, I would like to say that the paper is perfectly well written: the story is very clear and the mathematical writing is precise and fluid. This made the reading very pleasant and I would like to thank the authors for that.

The aim of the author is clear: provide an (under certain constraints) optimal linear predictor that may perform better than the minimum norm one naturally considered in the literature (naturally coming from GD or SGD on the empirical risk). The authors do so quite effortlessly ad give, to my opinion a nice result for those asking whether minimum norm solutions are always good estimators. **Yet**, I would have liked a lot more comments on the shape of this estimator: what intuition should the reader build when reading the paper ? A clear weakness is that the authors never really take the time of analysing why makes the estimator optimal (beyond technical considerations). In the very same manner, Proposition 2 gives that the optimal linear response is in fact the solution recovered by GD with a certain initialization: could the authors comment a bit on this ? 

The result on the divergence of the minimum norm linear interpolator (whereas the optimal one does not) is nice: however I would really need to understand further *why* and *when* we should expect this to happen. And in general cases *how close* should I expect the minimum norm interpolator to be close to the optimal one ? 


**Minor comments:**

- I guess that the part on the random matrix concepts arrive too early. As far as I understood, there is absolutely no need to put this part at the beginning, and this could be postpone in section 4.2 or even in the Appendix. 

- The part on random features model gives nothing new to the results: they are not commented (at all) and are absolutely expected once the linear model is covered. If these results are not further commented, then I recommend to put it in the Appendix as they do not give any additional insight on the paper. A nice introduction on why linear model are trendy is enough in my opinion (NTK, random features...)

- In Section 4.2, is this possible to derive a close form for the asymptotic risk of the optimal interpolator ? If not, why is this complicated ?

- Give more motivation and justification for the form of the *optimal linear interpolating estimator* you build. Why are you okay with the dependance on \Sigma, \phi, \delta. How could relax these ? I understand that these parameter will be estimated on the fly later: but once again, the comments on this are not deep precise enough to convince me. In particular there are only a few comments on the Graphiical Lasso approximation and why this is a good idea to approximate the population covariance.

- Plots commenting Proposition 2 would rely complement the result! If not, it seems not so important: a comparison with GD without preconditionning would be welcomed.


**Conclusion**

Even if I really enjoyed reading the paper, my opinion is that, under this form, the results lack from commentaries, intuition, insights and a bit of further developments.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes

More precisely the authors derive an explicit formula for the *best* linear interpolator that can be written as a linear function of the outputs and as a function of desired quantities. From this, they show how to recover theoretically this estimator from a optimization algorithm. Finally they expose cases where the designed estimator show some good generalization properties while the miminum norm interpolator does not.  The link with kernel methods is also made.

**Overall comments and impression**

And in general cases *how close* should I expect the minimum norm interpolator to be close to the optimal one ? 


**Minor comments:**

- I guess that the part on the random matrix concepts arrive too early. As far as I understood, there is absolutely no need to put this part at the beginning, and this could be postpone in section 4.2 or even in the Appendix. 

- The part on random features model gives nothing new to the results: they are not commented (at all) and are absolutely expected once the linear model is covered. I understand that these parameter will be estimated on the fly later: but once again, the comments on this are not deep precise enough to convince me. In particular there are only a few comments on the Graphiical Lasso approximation and why this is a good idea to approximate the population covariance.

reviews.text:
# Peer Review - Rewritten

## Summary

This manuscript investigates the problem of finding optimal linear interpolation methods. The authors derive an analytical expression for the *optimal* linear interpolator as a function of observed outputs and target quantities. They subsequently demonstrate how this estimator can be recovered through an optimization algorithm framework. Additionally, the paper identifies scenarios where their proposed estimator exhibits superior generalization compared to the minimum norm interpolator. The authors also establish connections to kernel methods literature.

## Overall Assessment and Comments

I should note at the outset that my background is not primarily in statistical learning theory, so there may be nuanced insights that I have not fully appreciated.

I must commend the authors on the exceptional clarity of exposition: the narrative flows logically, the mathematical presentation is rigorous yet accessible, and the paper is genuinely enjoyable to read. The authors deserve recognition for this quality of presentation.

The paper's central contribution is clear and valuable: the authors propose an (under specific constraints) superior linear predictor that can outperform the standard minimum norm solution typically arising from gradient descent or stochastic gradient descent on empirical risk. The authors accomplish this objective convincingly and provide what I consider an insightful answer to the important question of whether minimum norm solutions are universally optimal. **However**, the manuscript would benefit substantially from deeper discussion regarding the structure and interpretation of this estimator: what conceptual understanding should readers develop from this work? A notable limitation is the insufficient analysis of *why* the estimator achieves optimality beyond the mathematical derivations. Similarly, Proposition 2 establishes that the optimal linear response corresponds to the gradient descent solution under particular initialization conditions—the authors would strengthen their contribution by elaborating on the implications of this connection.

The demonstration that minimum norm linear interpolators diverge while optimal ones remain bounded is interesting; nonetheless, I would appreciate clarification on *why* and *under what conditions* we should anticipate this behavior. More broadly, practitioners need guidance on *how closely* the minimum norm interpolator approximates the optimal solution in typical scenarios.

## Minor Issues and Suggestions

- The random matrix theory material appears prematurely in the exposition. Based on my reading, there is no compelling reason to position this content at the outset; it could logically be relocated to Section 4.2 or the appendix without disrupting the narrative flow.

- The random features model section contributes limited novelty: the results follow predictably from the linear model analysis and receive minimal discussion. Unless this section receives substantive additional commentary, I recommend moving it to the appendix, as it does not enhance the paper's core insights. A brief motivational paragraph on the relevance of linear models (via connections to neural tangent kernels and random features) would suffice.

- In Section 4.2, can the authors derive a closed-form expression for the asymptotic risk of their optimal interpolator? If not, what technical obstacles prevent this? This explanation would be valuable.

- The paper would benefit from stronger motivation and justification for the specific form of the *optimal linear interpolating estimator*. Why is the dependence on Σ, φ, and δ appropriate? What possibilities exist for relaxing these dependencies? While the authors mention that these parameters will be estimated empirically, the discussion lacks sufficient depth and rigor to be fully persuasive. Particularly, the treatment of graphical lasso approximation for population covariance estimation deserves more thorough justification and commentary.

- Visualizations illustrating Proposition 2 would substantially strengthen the presentation. Alternatively, if such plots are deemed unnecessary, then the result's practical importance may be questionable; in this case, comparisons with unaccelerated gradient descent would be valuable additions.

## Conclusion

While I genuinely appreciated the reading experience and found the core contributions sound, the manuscript in its current form would be strengthened by expanded commentary, clearer intuitive explanations, and deeper analytical development of the key results.

## Limitations and Societal Impact

Addressed adequately.

## Recommendation

**6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold**

reviews.summary: This paper considers the problem of optimal interpolation in linear regression. It defines the class of response-linear achievable interpolators, and provides a closed-form expression for the optimal interpolator in this class. The paper is about an important topic in machine learning and statistics. However, it is unclear to me whether the "achievable" interpolators are really achievable, and as a result if the investigation is meaningful.
reviews.main_review:
Major comments and questions:
1. The paper claims that the realization of true data-generating parameter and the realization of the noise in the training data are unknown. However, it assumes that the population covariance, the signal-to-noise ratio, and the covariance of the prior for the signal are known. The latter seems to be more unrealistic. For high dimensional data, the covariance matrix contains much more number of parameters than the regression coefficient so it is a much harder problem to estimate the covariance matrix. In a Bayesian setting, the primary goal is to find the distribution of the parameter. If the distribution of the parameter is assumed to be known, then there is no estimation problem.

2. For $\Phi$, do you mean it is the correlation matrix? Otherwise calling $r^2$ the signal is not appropriate. 
   
3. The population risk between lines 125 and 126 is not clear.It is written as the conditional expectation given $X, \xi, w^*$, but this does not make sense. A risk should not be random.

4. I think the $y$ in line 131 should be $y_i$, but what is the $y$ in line 132? The $y$ in line 117 should be $y_i$? How about the $y$ in lines 120 and 124? Each of these may be typos or poor notations, but when there are too many this type of problems and makes it hard to following the paper it becomes a major issue. 
   
5. Line 142, I believe you need $\gamma\ge1$.

6. Line 156 says "rank(X) = n with probability 1" while this becomes a condition in line 160.

7. Definition 4, do you mean there exists a known function? Otherwise, the function $f$ may depends further on other unknowns.

8. As in my first point, the $w_O$ is not realistic. The updating formula in (7) requires the inverse of $\Sigma$ which is almost impossible for high dimensional data. 

9. I don't think Hastie et al. (2019) considered the case of $\Phi=I_d$. It is probably a typo here.
   

Minor issues and typos:

Line 53, "are best" to "are the best".

In (5), use paratheses to make the definition clear.

reviews.text:
# Peer Review - Rewritten

## Summary

The paper investigates optimal interpolation within the framework of linear regression, introducing the concept of response-linear achievable interpolators and deriving a closed-form solution for the optimal member of this class. While the subject matter addresses a relevant problem in machine learning and statistics, a fundamental concern exists regarding whether the proposed "achievable" interpolators can actually be realized in practice, which raises questions about the practical significance of the results.

## Major Comments and Questions

1. **Unrealistic Assumptions About Known Quantities**: The paper posits that the true data-generating parameter and training noise realizations are unknown, yet simultaneously assumes knowledge of the population covariance matrix, the signal-to-noise ratio, and the prior covariance for the signal. This latter set of assumptions appears considerably less plausible than the former. In high-dimensional settings, the covariance matrix is parameterized by substantially more parameters than the regression coefficients themselves, making its estimation a significantly more challenging problem. Within a Bayesian framework, the primary objective typically involves characterizing the parameter distribution. If this distribution is already assumed to be known a priori, the estimation problem becomes vacuous.

2. **Notation and Definition Ambiguity for $\Phi$**: The paper should clarify whether $\Phi$ denotes a correlation matrix. If it does not, then the terminology used to describe $r^2$ as "the signal" warrants reconsideration.

3. **Unclear Definition of Population Risk**: The formulation of population risk presented between lines 125 and 126 lacks clarity. The expression is given as a conditional expectation conditioned on $X, \xi, w^*$, which is problematic because a risk measure should not itself be a random quantity.

4. **Inconsistent and Ambiguous Notation**: Multiple instances of notational inconsistency appear throughout the manuscript. Line 131 appears to require $y_i$ rather than $y$; the meaning of $y$ on line 132 is unclear; and lines 117, 120, and 124 similarly seem to conflate $y$ with $y_i$. While individual instances might be dismissed as minor typos, the cumulative effect of such notational imprecision significantly impedes readability and comprehension.

5. **Missing Constraint**: The derivation on line 142 appears to require the constraint $\gamma \geq 1$.

6. **Inconsistent Treatment of Rank Condition**: Line 156 asserts that "rank(X) = n with probability 1," whereas this statement is subsequently treated as a formal condition in line 160. This inconsistency requires clarification.

7. **Ambiguity in Definition 4**: Definition 4 should specify whether it presupposes the existence of a known function. Without such specification, the function $f$ could depend on additional unknown quantities, rendering the definition incomplete.

8. **Impractical Requirements for Computing $w_O$**: As mentioned in point 1, the quantity $w_O$ is not practically realizable. The update formula in equation (7) requires computing the inverse of $\Sigma$, which is computationally infeasible in high-dimensional contexts.

9. **Potential Citation Error**: The reference to Hastie et al. (2019) in connection with the case $\Phi=I_d$ appears questionable. This may represent a typographical error requiring verification.

## Minor Issues and Typos

- Line 53: Change "are best" to "are the best"
- Equation (5): Add parentheses to clarify the definition

## Limitations and Societal Impact

The concerns raised in the main review section address the limitations of this work.

## Overall Assessment

The paper tackles a topic of relevance to the machine learning and statistics communities; however, the fundamental assumptions regarding what information is known versus unknown appear misaligned with practical reality. The notational inconsistencies throughout the manuscript further detract from clarity. Most critically, the practical achievability of the proposed interpolators remains questionable, which undermines the significance of the theoretical results.

## Recommendation

**Rating: 4 - Acceptable in scope but insufficient in execution - Rejection**

The manuscript requires substantial revision to address the unrealistic assumptions, clarify the notation, and convincingly demonstrate the practical relevance of the proposed framework before it would be suitable for publication.

title: Differentiable rendering with perturbed optimizers

reviews.summary:
This paper proposes a new differentiable renderer by introducing perturbed optimizers. The paper re-writes the non-differentiable rasterization and aggregation steps to heaven side functions and relaxes them to be differentiable by adding noise. 
The author shows that previous work, especially softras, can be treated as a special case of the perturbed renderer with specific prior selections. The authors also demonstrated in several experiments they achieve better or comparable results than the state of the art.
reviews.main_review:
Strengths

1) Differentiable reformulation
This paper treats rasterization as a linear programing problem.  It extends the [4] to the rendering pipeline and relaxes the non-differentiable rendering to be differentiable by introducing perturbed optimizers. It provides a new differentiable renderer from a new perspective.

2) Better performance
The authors show AS and VR are very useful in optimization, for both perturbed renderer and softras. In the 6D pose comparison, the results are better than softras.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact:
There are several weak points of this paper.

1) The paper is not self-contained.  I have a hard time understanding each equation. While the authors largely rely on [4], it makes readers hard to follow without knowing [4]. Moreover, the notations in this paper are very vague.   E.g. line 66 it should be j \in [1,.., m]?  line 76 z should be depth order?  If it is depth, how do you compute <z, y>?  line 88, what's v?  The paper writing definitely needs to be improved.


2) Relation of softras and noise types.  The authors show that softras can be treated as the special case of the perturbed renderer. Softras has some hyperparameters which are hard to explain but in the new renderer, all the parameters have clear noise intensity meaning.  While this is very good, but the paper lacks further analysis of which noise should we use. E.g. in table 1, it seems Gaussian noise is the best. But could it be generalized to other cases? 


3) Comparison.  In line 214 the authors said, "Unless stated, the results for the other renderers are extracted from their original publications, as we were not able to get access to their implementations."  I am a little confused about this.  Clearly, N3mr, softras, dibr released their renderers. Thus, why not simply replace the renderer from perturbed renderer to new renderers and compare the score?  Directly copying scores from others' papers may have the wrong comparison. While all the methods use shapenet, the rendered images could vary from lighting to camera views. I would highly recommend the authors redo the comparison.


4)  The authors assume each pixel will be influenced by all the triangles. For each pixel, it renders the influences of all the triangles and combines them together(Eq 3).  I wonder what's the rendering speed of the perturbed renderer? Theoretically, such design plus the MC sampling will make the renderer very time-consuming.  The authors should show the rendering speed comparison.


5) Following the last concern.  Generally speaking, rasterization accelerates the rendering speed by assuming each pixel is influenced by the frontest triangle.  On the other side, MC is always used in ray tracing to compute multiple bounces. The authors introduce MC in rasterization, which seems to adopt the weak points of two rendering methods.   I wonder did the authors consider extending it to RT?

Post rebuttal:

The authors addressed my concerns and I will raise the score to be marginally above the threshold and be happy to see this paper get accepted.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

reviews.text:
Summary:
This work introduces a novel differentiable rendering framework built upon perturbed optimizers. The core contribution involves reformulating the typically non-differentiable processes of rasterization and pixel aggregation as differentiable operations through the introduction of stochastic perturbations. The authors demonstrate that existing methods, particularly SoftRas, represent special instances of their more general framework with particular noise configurations. Experimental validation indicates competitive or superior performance relative to contemporary approaches.

Main Review:

Strengths

1) Novel theoretical perspective on differentiable rendering
The paper frames rasterization within a linear programming context and extends prior work [4] to construct a differentiable rendering pipeline. By employing perturbed optimizers to relax non-differentiable operations into smooth, differentiable ones, the authors present an innovative conceptual approach to this challenging problem.

2) Empirical improvements and general applicability
The authors effectively demonstrate that adaptive sampling (AS) and variance reduction (VR) techniques provide consistent benefits across both their proposed renderer and SoftRas. Notably, their 6D pose estimation experiments show measurable improvements over SoftRas performance.

Limitations:

1) Clarity and self-sufficiency concerns
The paper lacks sufficient self-contained exposition. Individual equations are difficult to parse without intimate familiarity with reference [4], creating a significant barrier to accessibility. Additionally, notation throughout the manuscript requires clarification. For instance: at line 66, the index constraint for j appears incomplete; at line 76, the meaning of z (presumably depth ordering) is ambiguous, and the inner product computation <z, y> requires explanation; at line 88, the definition of v is missing. The overall presentation quality would benefit substantially from revision and improved clarity.

2) Incomplete analysis of noise model selection
While the authors convincingly establish that SoftRas emerges as a special case of their framework—thereby providing principled interpretations for previously opaque hyperparameters—the work lacks systematic guidance on noise distribution selection. Table 1 suggests Gaussian noise performs optimally, yet the paper provides no analysis of whether this conclusion generalizes across different problem domains or whether alternative noise distributions might be preferable under specific conditions.

3) Experimental comparison methodology concerns
The statement at line 214 regarding extraction of baseline results from prior publications raises methodological questions. Given that implementations of N3MR, SoftRas, and DIBR are publicly available, a more rigorous approach would involve reimplementing these methods with your proposed renderer to ensure fair comparison. Relying on reported scores from other papers introduces potential confounds stemming from differences in rendering parameters, lighting conditions, and camera configurations, even when all methods evaluate on the same dataset. I strongly encourage the authors to conduct direct, controlled comparisons.

4) Computational efficiency analysis is missing
The framework assumes every pixel receives contributions from all triangles, with rendering computed through Monte Carlo aggregation (Eq. 3). This architectural choice, combined with MC sampling requirements, suggests potentially significant computational overhead. The paper should include explicit rendering speed comparisons against baseline methods to characterize practical efficiency implications.

5) Conceptual tension with rendering paradigms
Traditional rasterization achieves efficiency by assuming each pixel is affected only by the nearest triangle. Conversely, Monte Carlo sampling is characteristic of ray tracing for computing multi-bounce light transport. Your approach combines MC sampling with rasterization, seemingly inheriting computational drawbacks from both paradigms while mitigating neither. Have the authors considered extending this framework toward ray tracing instead, where MC sampling is more naturally suited?

Post-rebuttal assessment:

The authors provided satisfactory responses to the raised concerns. Given these clarifications, I am adjusting my evaluation upward and would support acceptance of this work.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a new differentiable rendering method, differentiable perturbed renderers,  wherein the gradients of the rasterization step are approximated using perturbed optimizers. They implement this change on top of the SoftRas method and demonstrate that it leads to better pose estimation, and on par performance with state of the art methods in unsupervised single image 3d object reconstruction.
reviews.main_review:
This is a strong paper. It is written clearly, and the method is explained properly. Perturbed optimizers seem to be a new way for tackling optimization over discrete operations in deep learning, and their application to rasterization is well motivated and appropriate. The theory required for applying them to this new setting seems right, and is well explained. The results are also adequate for the claims made. It would have been nice to see higher performance on the reconstruction task, though diff rendering is a relatively new and so highlighting new avenues to explore , such as your proposed perturbed optimizers,  is important, and so I do not believe it is necessary.

 I would say one nice set of experiments to see would be optimization over the same set of parameters as in the DIB-R  paper Figure 2. ( camera position, texture , vertex positions, etc. ), with qualitative and quantitative comparisons to DIB-R and SoftRas. Having this would go a long was to increasing my score, and I believe would sell you paper quite a bit better. The pose estimation seems quite a bit easier than optimizing for texture and vertex position for example, and it would be nice to see how you method fairs, even if the performance is only on par or worse.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes.

Summary:
This paper proposes a new differentiable rendering method, differentiable perturbed renderers,  wherein the gradients of the rasterization step are approximated using perturbed optimizers. They implement this change on top of the SoftRas method and demonstrate that it leads to better pose estimation, and on par performance with state of the art methods in unsupervised single image 3d object reconstruction.

Main Review:
This is a strong paper. It is written clearly, and the method is explained properly. Perturbed optimizers seem to be a new way for tackling optimization over discrete operations in deep learning, and their application to rasterization is well motivated and appropriate. The theory required for applying them to this new setting seems right, and is well explained. The results are also adequate for the claims made. It would have been nice to see higher performance on the reconstruction task, though diff rendering is a relatively new and so highlighting new avenues to explore , such as your proposed perturbed optimizers,  is important, and so I do not believe it is necessary.

 I would say one nice set of experiments to see would be optimization over the same set of parameters as in the DIB-R  paper Figure 2. ( camera position, texture , vertex positions, etc. ), with qualitative and quantitative comparisons to DIB-R and SoftRas. Having this would go a long was to increasing my score, and I believe would sell you paper quite a bit better. The pose estimation seems quite a bit easier than optimizing for texture and vertex position for example, and it would be nice to see how you method fairs, even if the performance is only on par or worse.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Yes.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
# Rewritten Peer Review

## Summary

The authors introduce a novel differentiable rendering approach that leverages perturbed optimizers to approximate gradients during the rasterization process. Building upon the SoftRas framework, they demonstrate improvements in pose estimation tasks and achieve competitive results with existing methods for unsupervised 3D object reconstruction from single images.

## Main Review

This manuscript represents solid work that is well-articulated and methodologically sound. The authors present perturbed optimizers as an innovative approach to handling optimization challenges arising from discrete operations within deep learning pipelines, and their application to the rasterization component is both theoretically justified and practically motivated. The mathematical foundations underlying the extension of perturbed optimizers to this novel domain appear correct and are presented with sufficient clarity. The experimental validation adequately supports the paper's central claims. While one might hope for stronger quantitative results on the reconstruction benchmark, it is important to recognize that differentiable rendering remains an emerging research area. In this context, introducing novel optimization strategies such as the perturbed optimizer framework is a valuable contribution, and I do not consider the current performance levels to be a significant limitation.

A particularly valuable addition would be a comprehensive experimental comparison following the evaluation protocol established in the DIB-R paper Figure 2, which would involve joint optimization of multiple parameters including camera position, texture, and vertex coordinates. Including both qualitative and quantitative comparisons against DIB-R and SoftRas baselines would substantially strengthen the paper's impact and provide compelling evidence for the method's utility. The current pose estimation experiments, while demonstrating clear benefits, represent a relatively constrained optimization problem compared to the more challenging multi-parameter scenarios such as joint texture and vertex optimization. Demonstrating how the proposed method performs in these more complex settings—whether matching, exceeding, or falling short of competing approaches—would be highly persuasive and would meaningfully enhance the paper's contribution.

## Limitations and Societal Impact

Addressed adequately.

## Rating

**7: Good paper, accept**

reviews.summary:
This paper presents a method for converting a standard non-differentiable rasterization-based renderer into a differentiable counterpart using recent advances in the perturbed optimizer. The main contribution lies in the generic nature provided by the theoretical formulation, where some specifically chosen priors in the proposed framework can retrieve recent state-of-the-art differentiable renderers, e.g. SoftRas. 
As the proposed approach mainly relies on a Monte-Carlo estimator for generating the smoothing parameters. Variance-reduction mechanism and sensitivity analysis are also incorporated into the proposed framework to ensure the robustness of the approximate renderer. Experiments on pose estimation and single-view reconstruction are conducted for comparing the performance of the proposed method and previous state-of-the-art approaches.
reviews.main_review:
Pros:

- By introducing the perturbed optimizer to analyzing the non-differentiability of standard rasterization-based rendering pipeline provides new insight into constructing a generic differentiable renderer. The perturbed differentiable rendering can also be implemented on top of existing renderers without major modifications, which makes this approach versatile and a potential performance booster for existing DR techniques.

Cons:

- In terms of technical novelty, it seems to be a direct application of perturbed optimizer to differentiable rendering. Though it requires some efforts (such as the variance reduction and sensitivity analysis) to adapt the existing theory to this specific application, it is not very challenging as there are existing protocols to follow.

- Evaluation-wise, in the task of single-view reconstruction, though the proposed method can achieve comparable performance compared to the state-of-the-art approaches, it still fails to outperform DIB-R in mean accuracy and also outperformed by SoftRas in many categories, such as the Dresser, Car, etc. The 3D pose estimation is only evaluated on a simple cube which cannot provide a very comprehensive measurement of the proposed approach.

This paper presents a method for converting a standard non-differentiable rasterization-based renderer into a differentiable counterpart using recent advances in the perturbed optimizer. The main contribution lies in the generic nature provided by the theoretical formulation, where some specifically chosen priors in the proposed framework can retrieve recent state-of-the-art differentiable renderers, e.g. SoftRas.

Variance-reduction mechanism and sensitivity analysis are also incorporated into the proposed framework to ensure the robustness of the approximate renderer. Experiments on pose estimation and single-view reconstruction are conducted for comparing the performance of the proposed method and previous state-of-the-art approaches.

reviews.text:
# Rewritten Peer Review

## Summary

This work proposes a methodology for transforming standard non-differentiable rasterization-based rendering systems into differentiable variants by leveraging contemporary developments in perturbed optimization theory. The key innovation centers on the generality of the theoretical framework, which demonstrates that certain carefully selected design choices within the proposed approach can recover existing state-of-the-art differentiable renderers such as SoftRas. The approach relies fundamentally on Monte-Carlo estimation for deriving smoothing coefficients, with additional variance-reduction techniques and sensitivity analyses integrated to enhance stability and reliability. The proposed method is validated through experiments on pose estimation and monocular 3D reconstruction tasks.

## Main Review

**Strengths:**

- The introduction of perturbed optimization theory as a lens for understanding and addressing the non-differentiability inherent in standard rasterization pipelines offers a valuable theoretical perspective. A significant practical advantage is that the method can be layered onto existing rendering systems with minimal architectural changes, making it broadly applicable and potentially beneficial for enhancing current differentiable rendering frameworks.

**Weaknesses:**

- From a technical contribution standpoint, the work appears to constitute a relatively straightforward instantiation of existing perturbed optimizer theory applied to the differentiable rendering domain. While adaptation efforts such as variance reduction and sensitivity analysis are necessary, these refinements follow established methodological patterns and do not represent substantial technical innovation or complexity.

- The experimental validation reveals notable limitations. In single-view 3D reconstruction benchmarks, although the proposed method achieves results comparable to existing approaches, it does not consistently surpass prior work—specifically, it underperforms DIB-R in mean accuracy metrics and is outperformed by SoftRas across several object categories including Dresser and Car. Additionally, the 3D pose estimation evaluation is restricted to a basic cube scenario, which provides insufficient breadth to thoroughly assess the method's capabilities and generalizability across diverse conditions.

## Limitations and Societal Impact

Adequately addressed.

## Rating

**6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold**

The paper presents a theoretically grounded contribution with practical utility, but the limited novelty in technical execution and mixed empirical results position it at the borderline of acceptance.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a differentiable rendering method that builds connection between the 3D model and 2D perspective appearance. Different from previous methods, the differentiable rendering is formulated into an optimization problem. Two computer vision tasks, including pose estimation and 3D shape from color image, are evaluated with the proposed renderer and compared to SoftRas [22].
reviews.main_review:
Strength:
- The performance for pose estimation outperforms existing methods.
- The formulation seems novel.

Weakness:
- Could the proposed formulation be applied to other 3D shape representations, e.g. point cloud, implicit? If so, an experiment should be added. If not, then better to mention this is a mesh-specific technic.

- The algorithm seems to be computational costy, e.g. 1 min per image. Would this heavily affect the training efficiency? While comparing to other methods, e.g. SoftRas, the run-time efficiency and hyper-parameters, e.g. number of iteration, training epoch, should be mentioned. Otherwise the comparison may not be on a fair basis.

- Keeping the boundary differentiable is one of the main issues in existing differentiable rendering technics, which is also argued mainly in this paper. However it is not clear how this is improved by the proposed method. From Fig 2 and 4, it seems the boundary area has obvious artifacts. Also some experiments should be performed to highlight the improvements over occlusion boundary.

- The performance on 3D shape prediction does not outperform existing approaches. 

Overall, despite a novel formulation, the improvements over existing approaches are not clear. As shown in the experiment, the propose method does not necessarily produce more accurate rendering or support better for vision tasks. Considering that the proposed tech is also quite slow, the overall advantage over existing approaches is not very clear.

reviews.text:
# Rewritten Peer Review

## Summary

The authors introduce a differentiable rendering approach that establishes a connection between 3D geometry and 2D image observations through an optimization-based formulation. This differentiable renderer is evaluated on two computer vision applications—pose estimation and 3D shape reconstruction from single images—with comparisons primarily against SoftRas [22].

## Strengths

- Pose estimation results demonstrate competitive or superior performance relative to existing methods in the literature.
- The proposed mathematical formulation appears to introduce novel elements to the field.

## Weaknesses

- **Limited scope of applicability**: The work does not adequately address whether the proposed optimization-based formulation generalizes to alternative 3D representations such as point clouds or implicit functions. The authors should either provide experimental validation on diverse shape representations, or explicitly clarify that this approach is restricted to mesh-based geometry.

- **Computational efficiency concerns**: The reported computational cost of approximately one minute per image raises significant questions about practical applicability. The review lacks detailed discussion of how this impacts overall training time and convergence. Furthermore, when comparing against baseline methods like SoftRas, the paper omits crucial implementation details such as computational runtime, number of optimization iterations, and training epochs. Without these specifications, the experimental comparison cannot be considered fair or conclusive.

- **Insufficient improvement on boundary handling**: Given that managing differentiability at occlusion boundaries is positioned as a central contribution of this work, the actual improvements remain unclear. Visual inspection of Figures 2 and 4 reveals noticeable artifacts in boundary regions, suggesting the method may not adequately address this fundamental challenge. Dedicated experiments quantifying the boundary quality improvements relative to existing techniques would strengthen this claim.

- **Weak performance on shape reconstruction**: The method does not achieve state-of-the-art results on the 3D shape prediction task, limiting its utility for this application domain.

## Overall Assessment

While the paper presents an interesting formulation, the empirical evidence for its advantages over existing methods is unconvincing. The experimental results demonstrate mixed outcomes—strong performance on pose estimation but weaker results on shape reconstruction—and do not clearly establish that the proposed rendering approach produces superior quality or meaningfully advances vision task performance. Combined with the substantial computational overhead and unresolved questions about boundary handling, the practical benefits over current approaches remain insufficiently demonstrated.

## Limitations and Societal Impact

No additional comments.

## Recommendation

**4: Acceptable work with notable limitations, but not sufficiently compelling for acceptance**

This manuscript requires substantial revisions addressing computational efficiency, generalizability, and clearer empirical advantages before it would be suitable for publication at a top venue.

title: NAS-Bench-x11 and the Power of Learning Curves

reviews.summary:
This paper proposes another benchmark dataset for NAS, NAS-Bench-x11. Compared to the original benchmarks NAS-Bench-101, NAS-Bench-301 and NAS-Bench-NLP, NAS-Bench-x11 contains the full training
information for each candidate architecture especially the learning curves so that it allows multi-fidelity NAS algorithms

NAS-Bench-x11 is a surrogate benchmark, which trains a surrogate model to predict the learning curve of the vast amount of candidate architectures. Prediction of the learning curve can be accomplished by singular value decomposition and noise modeling.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:

The paper is well motivated and clearly written. The paper is clear about limitations too.

I think this paper will be valuable to the community.  The created NAS-bench-x11 contains the full training information of different architectures, which may make it more convenient to analyse NAS algorithms and promote further NAS algorithm design. The idea of predicting the learning curve is very interesting to me. 

The authors conducted extensive experiments to demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method.  

Weakness:

This paper is a completion of the previously created benchmarks. Providing learning curves is also considered before in NAS-Bench-201, which weakens the novelty of this paper. So I recommend a weak acceptence.

reviews.text:
# Rewritten Peer Review

## Summary

This manuscript introduces NAS-Bench-x11, a new benchmark dataset designed for neural architecture search. Building upon earlier benchmarking efforts including NAS-Bench-101, NAS-Bench-301, and NAS-Bench-NLP, the proposed benchmark distinguishes itself by incorporating comprehensive training dynamics for each architecture candidate, particularly full learning curve trajectories. This feature enables the evaluation and development of multi-fidelity NAS approaches. The authors employ a surrogate modeling strategy that leverages singular value decomposition and noise modeling techniques to predict learning curves across a large space of candidate architectures.

## Strengths

The manuscript is well-articulated and demonstrates clear motivation for the proposed work. The authors transparently acknowledge the limitations of their approach, which strengthens the overall presentation.

I believe this contribution will prove valuable to the NAS research community. The inclusion of complete training information across different architectures in NAS-Bench-x11 provides a useful resource for deeper analysis of NAS methodologies and should facilitate the development of novel NAS algorithms. The approach to learning curve prediction through the proposed methodology is intellectually compelling and innovative.

The experimental evaluation is thorough and comprehensive, with multiple studies validating the efficacy of the proposed approach.

## Weaknesses

This work should be understood as an incremental extension of previously established benchmarking frameworks. The incorporation of learning curve information, while valuable, has been explored in prior work—specifically in NAS-Bench-201—which somewhat limits the conceptual novelty of the contribution. Given this incremental nature relative to existing benchmarks, I recommend acceptance with reservations.

## Limitations and Societal Impact

Appropriately addressed.

## Recommendation

**6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold**

reviews.summary: Existing NAS benchmarks often only include the architecture performance at the final or some selected checkpoint epochs. However, per-epoch information is essential for developing and evaluating multi-fidelity NAS algorithms. This work proposed to fit a surrogate model to predict the learning curve given an architecture encoding. Utilizing the proposed predictor, the author extends three existing benchmarks NASBench-101, NASBench-301, and NASBench-NLP to include per-epoch accuracies. Further, to demonstrate the necessity of developing multi-fidelity algorithms for NAS, the author also describes a procedure to extend the single-fidelity algorithms to multi-fidelity ones by using learning curve extrapolation to filter out candidate proposals with partial training. Despite possible room for improvements on the performance of the learning curve predictor used, the proposed method would enable researchers to develop and rapidly test multi-fidelity NAS algorithms. I vote for acceptance.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:
1. The contribution of this paper is relevant to the NAS community and would accelerate research on multi-fidelity NAS algorithms.
2. The performance of the proposed learning curve predictor model is overall satisfactory.
3. The author adopted the proposed method to extend three existing benchmarks to include per-epoch information.

Weakness:
1. The author reported ranking correlation and KL divergence between the predicted learning curve and ground truth. The numbers seem reasonable, but the errors are still non-negligible. It is unclear how misleading these errors would be if someone tries to develop multi-fidelity algorithms by querying the proposed extended benchmark. How would the existing multi-fidelity NAS algorithms perform under the proposed benchmark, over ground-truth data? This might be doable for NASBench201 or NASBench111 (the version created without using information about the given 4 checkpoint epochs), as they provide ground-truth accuracies on at least some checkpoints?

Comments and Questions:
1. For NASBench-NLP, using the accuracy from the first few epochs as features significantly improves the learning curve predictor's performance. Can we also expect visible improvements on other spaces, if the info of the first few epochs is also provided?

2. You mentioned in para 355 that future surrogate works should save full learning curve info to avoid the need for any extra extensions. But surrogate benchmarks like NASBench-301 only train a small portion of the architectures fully, and the large majority part of the search space will be benched by the predictor. So it seems that such surrogate benchmark would still need to be extended with learning curve prediction anyway. Could you elaborate more on this?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes

Despite possible room for improvements on the performance of the learning curve predictor used, the proposed method would enable researchers to develop and rapidly test multi-fidelity NAS algorithms. reviews.text:
# Peer Review (Rewritten)

## Summary

This paper addresses a notable gap in existing NAS benchmarks by proposing a surrogate modeling approach to predict per-epoch learning curves from architecture encodings. The authors recognize that current benchmarks typically report only final or sparse checkpoint accuracies, which limits the development and evaluation of multi-fidelity NAS methods. Using their proposed learning curve predictor, they augment three major benchmarks—NASBench-101, NASBench-301, and NASBench-NLP—with dense per-epoch performance information. Additionally, the authors present a framework for converting single-fidelity search algorithms into multi-fidelity variants through learning curve extrapolation-based filtering. While there is room for refinement in the learning curve predictor's accuracy, this work would provide valuable infrastructure for the community to explore and benchmark multi-fidelity NAS approaches. I recommend acceptance.

## Strengths

1. The paper makes a timely and valuable contribution that directly addresses a practical need in the NAS community, facilitating accelerated development of multi-fidelity search algorithms.

2. The proposed learning curve predictor demonstrates reasonable overall performance across the tested benchmarks.

3. The authors successfully demonstrate the applicability of their approach by extending three distinct and widely-used benchmark suites with per-epoch information.

## Weaknesses

1. While the authors report ranking correlation and KL divergence metrics between predicted and actual learning curves, the reported error margins remain non-trivial. A critical gap exists in understanding the practical implications of these prediction errors: specifically, how much would these inaccuracies degrade the performance of multi-fidelity NAS algorithms trained on the extended benchmarks compared to algorithms using ground-truth data? This could potentially be evaluated on NASBench-201 or similar benchmarks (e.g., a variant like NASBench-111 created without leveraging the provided checkpoint information) that offer ground-truth accuracies at multiple training stages.

## Questions and Comments

1. The authors note that incorporating early-epoch accuracy as a feature substantially enhances predictor performance on NASBench-NLP. Would similar performance gains materialize across the other benchmark spaces if early-epoch information were similarly provided?

2. In Section 355, the authors suggest that future surrogate benchmarks should archive complete learning curve data to eliminate the need for post-hoc extensions. However, surrogate benchmarks such as NASBench-301 only fully train a limited subset of architectures, with the vast majority of the search space represented through predictions. This raises a question: wouldn't surrogate benchmarks inherently require learning curve extension through prediction regardless? Could you clarify this point further?

## Limitations and Societal Impact

Adequately addressed.

## Rating

**7: Good paper, accept**

reviews.summary: The authors describe a mechanism to predict learning curves which they use to generate surrogate benchmarks from existing NAS-Bench versions without learning curves. The authors explore different ways to create learning curve embeddings, different ways to predict these embeddings using the architecture representation as an input and different ways to model the training noise. Furthermore, the authors discuss a short study on using learning curve extrapolation in context of speeding-up NAS.
reviews.main_review:
The authors provide a good description of their method, alternative ways to solve each problem faced and support their decisions with empirical evidence.

My main concern is about the usefulness of this surrogate benchmark which has problems as partly discussed by the authors. It remains unclear what impact these problems would have.

The noise modelling in this paper leads to artifacts we would not see in real learning curves. First, it is very unlikely that the noise is architecture- and hyperparameter-independent. Second, given just the very few examples in Figure 1 we observe that the predicted learning curve in early epochs can already exceed to highest value of the entire learning curve (possibly due to very high noise levels).
In general, all surrogate benchmarks have the problem that they introduce some sort of bias which will not match what you would see in reality. Therefore, these datasets are more similar to synthetic datasets than real ones. They might be nice to prototype but I wouldn’t use them to evaluate NAS methods.

The experiment on combining learning curve extrapolation is a nice reminder for the NAS community to keep these approaches in mind when comparing different approaches. However, it provides no novelty since these methods have been used in the context of NAS already (see [1]).
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The authors describe a mechanism to predict learning curves which they use to generate surrogate benchmarks from existing NAS-Bench versions without learning curves. The authors explore different ways to create learning curve embeddings, different ways to predict these embeddings using the architecture representation as an input and different ways to model the training noise. Furthermore, the authors discuss a short study on using learning curve extrapolation in context of speeding-up NAS.

Main Review:
The authors provide a good description of their method, alternative ways to solve each problem faced and support their decisions with empirical evidence.

My main concern is about the usefulness of this surrogate benchmark which has problems as partly discussed by the authors. It remains unclear what impact these problems would have.

The noise modelling in this paper leads to artifacts we would not see in real learning curves. First, it is very unlikely that the noise is architecture- and hyperparameter-independent. Second, given just the very few examples in Figure 1 we observe that the predicted learning curve in early epochs can already exceed to highest value of the entire learning curve (possibly due to very high noise levels).
In general, all surrogate benchmarks have the problem that they introduce some sort of bias which will not match what you would see in reality. Therefore, these datasets are more similar to synthetic datasets than real ones. They might be nice to prototype but I wouldn’t use them to evaluate NAS methods.

The experiment on combining learning curve extrapolation is a nice reminder for the NAS community to keep these approaches in mind when comparing different approaches. However, it provides no novelty since these methods have been used in the context of NAS already (see [1]).

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Yes

Rating:
5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Rewritten Peer Review

## Summary

This work presents a methodology for forecasting learning curves, which the authors leverage to construct surrogate benchmarks based on existing NAS-Bench datasets that lack learning curve information. The paper investigates multiple approaches for learning curve representation, various strategies for predicting these representations conditioned on architectural features, and distinct techniques for modeling training-related noise. Additionally, the authors conduct a preliminary investigation into the potential of learning curve extrapolation as a tool for accelerating NAS procedures.

## Main Review

The manuscript offers a clear exposition of the proposed methodology, thoughtfully considers alternative approaches to address each technical challenge encountered, and substantiates these design choices through empirical validation.

However, my primary reservation concerns the practical utility of the resulting surrogate benchmark, particularly given the limitations that the authors themselves acknowledge but do not fully resolve. Specifically, it remains ambiguous what practical consequences these limitations would entail.

The approach to noise modeling presented here produces artifacts that diverge from authentic learning curves. Two issues are particularly notable: First, the assumption that noise operates independently of both architectural characteristics and hyperparameter settings lacks realism. Second, the limited examples presented in Figure 1 demonstrate that predicted learning curves can surpass the maximum value observed across the entire training trajectory in early epochs, likely stemming from excessive noise injection.

More broadly, surrogate benchmarks inherently introduce systematic biases that do not reflect real-world phenomena. Consequently, these datasets function more akin to synthetic benchmarks than empirical ones. While such constructs may prove valuable for preliminary exploration and prototyping purposes, their reliability for rigorous evaluation of NAS methodologies is questionable.

The investigation into integrating learning curve extrapolation serves as a useful cautionary note for the NAS research community regarding the importance of considering such techniques during method comparison. Nonetheless, this contribution lacks novelty, as comparable approaches have already been explored and applied within the NAS domain (see [1]).

## Limitations and Societal Impact

Addressed appropriately.

## Rating

**5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold**

The work demonstrates technical competence and clear presentation, but the fundamental concerns regarding the reliability and applicability of the surrogate benchmark, combined with limited novelty in the extrapolation experiments, place this submission slightly below the bar for acceptance.

reviews.main_review:
## Positive points:
1. The paper presents a method to extend the existing single-fidelity benchmarks to multi-fidelity ones with full training information.
2. The authors create NAS-Bench-111, NAS-Bench-311, and NAS-Bench-NLP11 benchmark datasets, which may be beneficial to develop new NAS algorithm.

## Negative points:
1. Does the proposed method apply to real world NAS application? In practice, given a dataset, we may have no available training information of any architectures. In this case, how to apply the proposed method? It would be limited if the proposed method is only able to extend the existing NAS benchmarks.
2. In line 220, why do the authors sample architectures from BANANAS, local search and regularized evolution?
3. In line 225, the authors mention that the method is able to improve the performance with the accuracies from the tabular benchmark (at epochs 4, 12, 36, 108) as additional features. More details of this should be provided.
