Authors must justify why they can ignore this effect.

(RLM3) Figures need to be placed closer to the text where they’re referenced. 

(RLM4) Sometimes bolded numbers in tables are not the best results for that column. 

*Post rebuttal*: I thank the authors for their time and effort in crafting their response. A lot of my more technical comments were addressed. I am still very concerned about the extra capacity and don't feel these concerns are properly addressed by pointing out that one other baseline (IMTL-G) also has added capacity, and some of the new results actually may run counter to the proposed narrative - for example RotoGradNorm beating out RotoGrad. The method itself is well defined but I am still not sold on the scientific validity of the analysis. I will keep my rating a 4.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
More discussion could be provided regarding limitations and societal impact. In terms of the former, for example, there are plenty of benchmarks on which RotoGrad seems to not perform the best, so discussion of why needs to be more in detail.

Rating:
4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
reviews.text:
# Peer Review: RotoGrad

## Summary

This paper introduces RotoGrad, a multitask learning layer that applies rotations to shared feature representations in order to harmonize task gradients and reduce negative transfer. The approach operates on architectures featuring shared encoders with multiple task-specific heads, addressing both magnitude and directional misalignment among task gradients. The authors validate their approach through experiments across various computer vision benchmarks, comparing against established multitask learning baselines.

## Overall Assessment

The paper addresses a meaningful challenge in multitask learning and proposes a conceptually sound solution. While the method demonstrates potential and could prove valuable to the community if substantiated more rigorously, substantial gaps remain in the experimental validation and theoretical justification. The work would benefit from comprehensive ablation studies and clearer motivation for key design choices before I can be fully convinced of its effectiveness. That said, the core idea shows promise, and I encourage the authors to continue refining this direction.

## Strengths

(S1) The proposed method is presented clearly and intuitively, targeting a fundamental problem in effective multitask learning optimization.

(S2) The empirical results are encouraging, demonstrating improvements over competing baselines on the reported benchmarks.

(S3) Framing the approach as a Stackelberg game is a novel perspective that could offer broader insights into multitask learning, particularly given the prevalence of auxiliary loss optimization in modern MTL approaches.

(S4) The evaluation spans multiple architectural configurations and diverse datasets, strengthening the generalizability of the findings.

(S5) The authors have released code, supporting reproducibility and community validation.

## Major Concerns

(MC1) The motivation for modifying the forward pass rather than restricting interventions to the backward pass requires stronger justification. Prior work (e.g., Sinha et al., 2018) demonstrates that backward-pass-only methods can be equally effective. Either theoretical arguments or empirical comparisons are needed to establish why the more invasive approach of altering the shared feature space is necessary.

(MC2) The toy example lacks convincing power due to its limited scope—comparing only against vanilla SGD. More problematically, RotoGrad itself is optimized using RAdam while baselines rely on standard SGD, which introduces a confounding factor that artificially favors the proposed method.

(MC3) The performance gaps raise concerns about baseline implementations. Specifically, MGDA performs surprisingly poorly on CIFAR-based tasks despite being benchmarked successfully in the original MGDA paper. Such discrepancies with prior published results must be explained and reconciled before the current findings can be fully trusted.

(MC4) Computational overhead inconsistencies suggest potential implementation issues with baselines. GradNorm and GradDrop should have comparable computational costs since both require computing gradients with respect to all weights, yet the reported overheads differ substantially in CelebA experiments. Similarly, RotoGrad appears slower than PCGrad despite requiring backpropagation only to the feature layer rather than all parameters, which is counterintuitive and warrants investigation.

(MC5) Comprehensive ablation studies are essential to determine whether both the magnitude normalization and directional alignment components are individually necessary for the method's performance.

(MC6) The magnitude normalization component depends critically on network initialization, as it incorporates initial gradient magnitudes. The authors must clarify whether and how this dependency was addressed during implementation.

(MC7) On digit classification tasks, performance appears highly sensitive to RotoGrad layer dimensionality. Given the shallow network architectures used for these tasks (evident in the appendix), the contribution of added network capacity through the RotoGrad layer cannot be ruled out as a confounding factor.

## Minor Concerns

(LC1) Proposition 3.1, while mathematically correct, is relatively straightforward—if task gradients maintain positive pairwise similarity, their sum will reduce both losses for sufficiently small step sizes. This observation has been extensively discussed in prior MTL literature (MGDA, PCGrad), so independent treatment here adds limited novelty. Consider presenting this as intuitive reasoning rather than a formal proposition.

(LC2) The authors assert that homogenizing gradients at a single layer is equivalent to homogenizing gradients at weight layers. This claim is problematic because gradient propagation through subsequent layers will cause initially aligned gradients to diverge. The paper must justify why this effect can be neglected in the analysis.

(LC3) Figure placement should be improved—several figures appear distant from their corresponding text references, disrupting readability.

(LC4) Table formatting is inconsistent; some bolded entries do not correspond to the best performance in their respective columns.

## Post-Rebuttal Comments

I appreciate the authors' thorough response and acknowledge that several technical concerns were addressed. However, two issues remain unresolved. First, the concern regarding additional network capacity is not adequately dismissed by noting that one other baseline (IMTL-G) also introduces capacity—this does not isolate the effect of RotoGrad itself. Second, some of the new experimental results appear contradictory to the paper's narrative; notably, RotoGradNorm outperforming RotoGrad suggests that the claimed benefits of the core mechanism may not be as fundamental as presented. While the method is well-formulated, I remain unconvinced of its scientific validity based on the current analysis and evidence.

## Limitations and Societal Impact

The paper would benefit from more thorough discussion of its limitations. Notably, RotoGrad does not achieve state-of-the-art results across all tested benchmarks, yet the paper provides insufficient analysis of when and why the method underperforms. Additionally, explicit discussion of societal impact and potential negative applications would strengthen the submission.

## Recommendation

**Rating: 4 (Ok but not good enough - Rejection)**

The paper presents an interesting method with clear presentation, but insufficient experimental rigor and unresolved technical concerns prevent acceptance at this time. With substantial additional validation, ablation studies, and clarification of implementation details, this work could make a solid contribution to the multitask learning literature.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The work proposes RotoGrad, a multitask layer that rotates a shared feature space in a deep neural network to align gradients and mitigate negative transfer effects. The proposed method works within models with multiple prediction heads and a shared backbone, and deals both with magnitude and directional imbalance between task gradients. Authors provide multiple experiments in the computer vision space to show efficacy of the proposed method against other multitask baselines.

Main Review:
**Overall:** The paper presents an interesting new method to treat multitask learning systems. If robust, this method can have general use to the community and be a powerful addition to the toolbox. However, there are many unanswered questions and I remained overall unconvinced that the method works without major additional experimentation, including detailed ablation studies, and justifications (too many to address within the short rebuttal period in my opinion). I do think the method shows promise though, so regardless of the decision after this review process I hope the authors continue to work on their method.  

**Pros:**

(P1) Method is clear and intuitive, and tackles a crucial problem within optimal multitask training. 

(P2) Results seem promising and beat other baselines on benchmarks within the main paper. 

(P3) Formulation of the method as a Stackelberg game is interesting and can perhaps provide general insight into these problems, as many modern MTL methods involve optimizing auxiliary losses.

(P4) Results are given for multiple model structures and multiple datasets performing the same tasks. 

(P5) Code is provided for reproducibility. 

**Needs Comment/Revision (major):**

(RM1) Why must the proposed method interact with the forward pass? There are some other methods (see for example Sinha et al. at https://arxiv.org/abs/1806.08028) that propose methods which only interact with the backwards pass and seem to also work well. Either theoretical justification or additional experiments are needed to justify why doing the aggressive thing of altering the forward pass feature space is necessary. 

(RM2) The toy example provided is unconvincing, as the only comparison is to vanilla SGD. It is rather puzzling that even the Rotograd layer within the toy example is optimized using RAdam, which I feel would give Rotograd a severe advantage. 

(RM3) When the proposed method outperforms other methods that’s great, but when all the other methods perform very poorly it raises questions of whether the other methods are implemented correctly. For instance, why is MGDA performing so poorly on the CIFAR/MGDA tasks when the original MGDA paper used that as a benchmark and actually performed quite well? These discrepancies with the prior results need to be addressed for the current results to be fully believed.

(RM4) One strange detail is that GradNorm and GradDrop seem to have different compute overheads within the current implementations, at least within the CelebA experiments. However, as far as I can tell, GradNorm and GradDrop have essentially the same compute overhead (with the limiting factor in the compute overhead the same computation of dL_i/dw for each task loss L_i), so this discrepancy makes me concerned that the present implementations of baselines is not correct. Similarly, RotoGrad seems to be even slower than PCGrad, and PCGrad has to calculate dL_i/dw for all trainable weights while RotoGrad only needs to backpropagate to the feature layer, so this overhead is somewhat concerning as well.

(RM5) Ablation studies are needed to see whether both the magnitude and directional components of Rotograd are both needed. 

(RM6) The magnitude part of the implementation seems to include the initial gradient magnitude as part of the normalized quantity. However, this is incredibly dependent on the network initialization. Did authors account for this in some way? 

(RM7) The digit classification task performances seem to be very dependent on the size of the RotoGrad layer. Looking at the appendix, the network structure for these tasks is very shallow, so how can the authors rule out the effect of the added network capacity within the RotoGrad layer?

**Needs Comment/Revision (less major):**

(RLM1) Proposition 3.1 is correct but is rather obvious - would authors consider rephrasing into nonmathematical language to reduce potential confusion? If two gradients have positive pairwise similarity, then following their sum (for small enough learning rate) will clearly reduce both losses because loss reduction is step_size*(grad_k dot grad_sum). This proposition has also been heavily treated in prior work as well like MGDA and PCGrad, so does not necessarily need independent treatment here and can just be stated.

(RLM2) Authors claim that homogenizing gradients at one layer is equivalent to homogenizing gradients at the weight layers. This is a false statement, as chained gradient computations will cause even initially homogenized gradients at a feature layer to increasingly stray from each other. Authors must justify why they can ignore this effect.

(RLM3) Figures need to be placed closer to the text where they’re referenced. 

(RLM4) Sometimes bolded numbers in tables are not the best results for that column. 

*Post rebuttal*: I thank the authors for their time and effort in crafting their response. A lot of my more technical comments were addressed. I am still very concerned about the extra capacity and don't feel these concerns are properly addressed by pointing out that one other baseline (IMTL-G) also has added capacity, and some of the new results actually may run counter to the proposed narrative - for example RotoGradNorm beating out RotoGrad. The method itself is well defined but I am still not sold on the scientific validity of the analysis. I will keep my rating a 4.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
More discussion could be provided regarding limitations and societal impact. In terms of the former, for example, there are plenty of benchmarks on which RotoGrad seems to not perform the best, so discussion of why needs to be more in detail.

Rating:
4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
reviews.original_rating: 4: Ok but not good enough - rejection

title: CobBO: Coordinate Backoff Bayesian Optimization with Two-Stage Kernels

reviews.summary: The authors have proposed a new algorithm for black box optimization called CobBO. At a fundamental level, the algorithm is the extension of classic block coordinate ascent to black box optimization using techniques from Bayesian Optimization. The algorithm focuses on a small subset of coordinates in every iteration. In the first step, it uses a simple kernel to interpolate the values of the function learnt at other points to the points in the subspace. In the next step, it uses a full fledged BO with a more sophisticated kernel in the subset to learn the function in this subspace. It balances exploration and exploitation between subspaces by using a backoff stopping rule. The authors have supported their case by extensive empirical studies and some theoretical results.
reviews.main_review:
Pros:
- The paper proposes an interesting algorithm to address the troubles that usually BO based methods face in high dimensional problems. While the basic concept of block coordinate ascent to address high dimensionality is already known and well studied, the authors apply this for BO and take care of several aspects to make it a practically usable algorithm.
- The empirical evaluation of the algorithm seems quite extensive.

Questions for the authors:
- The paper mentions that the scale of RBF is not tuned and is fixed before hand. I am curious as to how this choice affects the results. It is clear that one cannot set an arbitrary value as it might lead  to over smoothing or under smoothing. So is there a range where it works well and how is it chosen? Also how does this choice affect the results?
- While the algorithm is clearly set in the noiseless regime (which is fine), I am curious as to what will happen in the noisy regime? Clearly some of aspects cannot be directly taken over since we would not have access to exact function values. How robust do you expect the algorithm to be to noise in the current version (only allowing for noisy updates of posterior and no additional adaption) ? I am curious because typically one would expect noise in real world scenarios and if the algorithm is sensitive it can break under the noise.
- The description of Sec 3.1 and 3.2 is unclear. There are clarifying details in Appendix C, but overall I could not find one description of the algorithm where the choice of $C_t$ and the backoff is explicitly stated, as used in the experiments. If different versions outlined in Appendix C are used in different experiments, depending on the task, I don't think the comparison is fair since CobBO is allowed to make some changes depending on the function but others are not. I think it would be really helpful and clear if the stopping rule and choice of subspace is exactly mentioned in the main paper.
- Could you elaborate the intuition behind the description of the stopping rule outlined in Appendix C? It seems like too precise a definition without sufficient description as to how the specific form was obtained.
- Is the value $|C_t|$ chosen uniformly at random in $\{1,2, \dots, 30\}$? 
- In the ablation study, when only of one parameters is studied, what is the value of the other ones which are kept constant?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

reviews.text:
# Peer Review - Rewritten

## Summary

This paper introduces CobBO, a novel black box optimization algorithm that extends classical block coordinate ascent methodology to the Bayesian optimization setting. The core approach partitions the optimization problem into lower-dimensional coordinate subspaces, processing a limited subset of variables at each iteration. The method employs a two-stage process: first, it leverages a simple kernel-based interpolation scheme to estimate function values from previously observed points onto the current subspace; subsequently, it applies sophisticated Bayesian optimization techniques with an expressive kernel to optimize within that subspace. The algorithm manages the exploration-exploitation tradeoff across different subspaces through a backoff-based termination criterion. The work is substantiated by comprehensive empirical validation and theoretical analysis.

## Main Review

**Strengths:**
- The paper tackles a significant challenge in Bayesian optimization—the curse of dimensionality—by adapting the well-established block coordinate ascent framework to this setting. Although coordinate-based decomposition has been previously explored, the authors thoughtfully integrate multiple components to create a practical and functional algorithm.
- The experimental evaluation is thorough and comprehensive in scope.

**Concerns and Questions:**

- The paper indicates that the RBF kernel scale parameter remains fixed and is not adaptively tuned. This raises important questions about parameter sensitivity: What range of values proves effective in practice? How was the specific choice determined? What is the magnitude of performance variation as this parameter is modified?

- The algorithm operates under the noiseless assumption, which is reasonable but limits applicability. Given that real-world optimization problems typically involve noisy observations, how would the current algorithm perform in such settings? Specifically, without access to exact function evaluations, which algorithmic components would require modification? How sensitive is the method to noise when only posterior updates are affected without further algorithmic adjustments?

- The descriptions in Sections 3.1 and 3.2 lack sufficient clarity. While additional details appear in Appendix C, no single coherent account of the algorithm exists that explicitly specifies how $C_t$ is determined and how the backoff mechanism operates as implemented in the experiments. If different algorithmic variants from Appendix C are employed for different experimental tasks, this raises fairness concerns regarding the comparison, as CobBO would have task-specific flexibility that baseline methods do not possess. The main paper would benefit from an explicit and complete statement of the stopping criterion and subspace selection procedure.

- The stopping rule presented in Appendix C appears overly technical without sufficient intuitive justification. Could you provide more explanation regarding the derivation and motivation behind this specific formulation?

- Is the cardinality $|C_t|$ selected uniformly at random from the set $\{1, 2, \ldots, 30\}$?

- In the ablation studies examining individual parameters, what are the fixed values assigned to the remaining parameters that are held constant?

## Limitations and Societal Impact

Addressed appropriately.

## Recommendation

**Rating: 7 – Good paper, accept**

This work makes a solid contribution to black box optimization by effectively adapting coordinate descent principles to the Bayesian optimization framework with practical considerations. While the paper would benefit from addressing the clarity issues noted above and providing more thorough justification for design choices, the overall contribution is valuable and the experimental validation is convincing. The authors should revise to incorporate more explicit algorithmic descriptions and provide clearer exposition of key components, particularly regarding parameter selection and the backoff mechanism.

reviews.summary: This paper aims at making Bayesian optimization scalable to problems with large dimensional inputs. The authors propose an approach that selects block coordinates of the input space to explore at each iteration. The authors provide some theoretical results to support the proposed approach, and report experimental results to demonstrate that the proposed approach can achieve better performance than existing approaches.
reviews.main_review:
Originality: 
The main idea of the proposed approach is to use the multiplicative weights update method in Bayesian optimization. To my limited knowledge about Bayesian optimization, this is novel and thus the originality is reasonable. 


Quality:
While the approach seems reasonable and the experimental results look promising, I have the following concerns about the paper. 

- The way they cite some of the existing works is not very precise, and thus I don't have a good impression of the quality of the work. For instance, [6] and [7] are the same book;  [26] on inverse distance weighting is Wikipedia; [32] and [33] are the same paper; [40] on Matern kernels is a webpage of scikit learn; [50] on radial basis functions is a webpage of scipy; [61] and [62] are the same paper; [64] and [65] are the same paper. 

- In lines 32-34, the authors say ``always exactly using the values on the queried points as the conditional events may mismatch the function’s local landscape by overemphasizing the approximation accuracy of the global landscape.'' Similar arguments are made in lines 43-48. I feel confused to see this argument. In GP regression, we usually assume noisy outputs, and the use of a regularized matrix inversion (where the regularizer is given by the noise variance) incorporates this assumption by automatically smoothing the outputs. 

- In line 57, the authors say ``CobBO uses the Automatic Relevance Determination (ARD) Matérn 5/2 kernel.'' But it is not clear how the authors specify the weights of this ARD kernel, as this is also not described in the supplementary. And the authors do not explain the value of the length scale parameter in the kernel. 

- In line 12 of Algorithm 1, there exists an RBF interpolation, denoted by $R$. The authors should describe the explicit from this interpolator. While the authors seem to explain that the RBF interpolation and GP regression are different, they are essentially the same approach. As such, RBF interpolation requires the computational complexity cubic to the data size. This point should also be discussed.

- In line 14 of Algorithm 1, $\Omega_t$ denotes the subspace spanned by the block coordinates $C_t$. I am wondering about how the authors specify the values for the coordinates in $\Omega_t$ not selected in $C_t$. For instance, suppose the dimensions are $D = 4$ and $C_t = \{1, 2\}$. Then is $x \in \Omega_t$ given in the form $x = (x_1, x_2, 0, 0)$ by specifying the coordinates not in $C_t$ (i.e,. \{3, 4\}) to be zeros? This point is not clear from the presentation. If this is the case, the authors implicitly make an assumption about the optimal solution that some of their coordinates are zero. This is rather a strong assumption.

- In Section 3.3, the authors define ``regret'' in terms of the selection of the best coordinate block. This regret is different from the regrets defined in terms of the global optimizer, usually analyzed in the Bayesian optimization literature. Therefore, the theoretical results of the paper do not really provide a theoretical guarantee for the proposed approach in terms of the convergence to the global optimizer.  

- While the authors show the hyperparameter settings in Table 2 of the supplementary, some information is still missing, such as the acquisition function used and the length scales of the kernels in RBF interpolation and GP regression. 


Clarity:
- Since the authors omit the concrete expressions for many mathematical concepts (e.g., RBF interpolation and the subspace $\Omega_t$), it is not very easy to deeply understand the mechanism of the proposed algorithm. Also, some key information for reproducing the experimental results is missing. 

Significance:
- There are many hyperparameters that need to be chosen in the proposed algorithm. (Table 2 in the supplementary shows an incomplete list of the hyperparameters.) If the performance of the proposed algorithm is sensitive to the choice of these parameters, the algorithm is not easy to be used by practitioners. This point is not clear from the paper. If this is the case, the significance of the proposed method is limited. 

- Also, as described above, it may be the case that the proposed approach implicitly assumes that some of the coordinates of the global optimizer are zeros. If this is the case, the proposed approach can only be applied to problems that satisfy this assumption, which may be restrictive.


------------ Post rebuttal --------------
As the authors made some clarifications, I have increased my score to 5. Still, however, I'm not very convinced by the argument of the authors on regret analysis.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: The authors do not discuss the limitations of their work.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

------------ Post rebuttal --------------
As the authors made some clarifications, I have increased my score to 5. Still, however, I'm not very convinced by the argument of the authors on regret analysis.

5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Peer Review: Scalable Bayesian Optimization via Block Coordinate Selection

## Summary

This work addresses the challenge of scaling Bayesian optimization to high-dimensional input spaces through a block coordinate selection strategy applied at each iteration. The authors integrate the multiplicative weights update method into their framework and provide theoretical analysis alongside empirical validation demonstrating improvements over competing methods.

## Originality

The incorporation of multiplicative weights update methodology into the Bayesian optimization setting represents a novel contribution to the field. Based on my familiarity with the Bayesian optimization literature, this direction appears sufficiently original and makes a reasonable contribution to the existing body of work.

## Quality

Although the core methodology appears sound and experimental outcomes are encouraging, several quality concerns warrant attention:

**Citation and Presentation Issues:**
The bibliography contains numerous problematic citations that raise concerns about the work's overall quality. Specifically: references [6] and [7] point to identical sources; [26] is sourced from Wikipedia rather than a peer-reviewed venue; [32] and [33] duplicate the same publication; [40] references a scikit-learn webpage instead of academic literature; [50] cites scipy documentation; and [61]/[62] as well as [64]/[65] are duplicate entries. These issues suggest insufficient care in literature documentation.

**Conceptual Clarity on Noise Handling:**
The authors argue in lines 32-34 and 43-48 that directly conditioning on queried values may distort the function's local structure by overweighting global accuracy. This reasoning is unclear to me. In standard Gaussian process regression, the framework naturally accommodates noisy observations through a noise variance parameter that regularizes matrix inversion, thereby automatically introducing appropriate smoothing without the stated concern.

**Kernel Specification Gaps:**
The paper mentions employing an Automatic Relevance Determination Matérn 5/2 kernel for CobBO (line 57) but fails to specify how the ARD weights are determined. Additionally, the length scale parameter values remain unexplained, and this information is absent from supplementary materials.

**RBF Interpolation and Computational Complexity:**
Algorithm 1, line 12 introduces RBF interpolation operator $R$ without explicit definition. While the authors distinguish RBF interpolation from GP regression conceptually, they are computationally equivalent approaches, both requiring cubic complexity in data size—a point requiring explicit discussion.

**Subspace Definition Ambiguity:**
The notation $\Omega_t$ in Algorithm 1, line 14 represents the subspace spanned by selected block coordinates $C_t$, but the handling of unselected dimensions remains unclear. For example, if $D=4$ and $C_t=\{1,2\}$, is the point $x \in \Omega_t$ constructed as $x=(x_1, x_2, 0, 0)$ with non-selected coordinates zeroed? If so, this implicitly assumes the optimal solution has zero values in certain coordinates—a restrictive assumption that should be made explicit.

**Regret Analysis Limitations:**
Section 3.3 defines regret relative to optimal block coordinate selection rather than global optimization, which diverges from standard Bayesian optimization regret definitions. Consequently, the theoretical guarantees do not establish convergence properties toward the global optimum, limiting their practical relevance.

**Missing Experimental Details:**
Table 2 of the supplementary material presents hyperparameter settings incompletely. Critical information remains absent, including the specific acquisition function employed and kernel length scale values for both RBF interpolation and GP regression components.

## Clarity

The presentation suffers from omitted mathematical specifications for key components—RBF interpolation formulation, precise definition of subspace $\Omega_t$, and others—making the algorithmic mechanism difficult to fully comprehend. Furthermore, essential details for experimental reproducibility are not provided.

## Significance

**Hyperparameter Sensitivity:**
The algorithm contains numerous hyperparameters, with Table 2 providing only a partial enumeration. The paper does not address whether performance exhibits substantial sensitivity to these choices. Should the method prove sensitive to parameter tuning, its practical utility for practitioners would be significantly compromised, potentially limiting impact.

**Restrictive Assumptions:**
As noted previously, if the approach implicitly assumes certain coordinates of the optimal solution are zero-valued, applicability becomes restricted to problems satisfying this constraint, reducing the method's generality and practical scope.

## Limitations and Societal Impact

The authors do not discuss limitations of their approach or potential societal implications.

## Post-Rebuttal Assessment

Following author clarifications, I revised my evaluation upward. However, I remain unconvinced by the authors' justification regarding the regret analysis framework and its relationship to global optimization guarantees.

## Overall Assessment

While the paper presents an interesting direction for scaling Bayesian optimization, the combination of citation quality issues, incomplete technical specifications, restrictive implicit assumptions, and theoretical guarantees disconnected from global optimization objectives prevent acceptance. The work requires substantial revision addressing clarity, completeness, and theoretical rigor before meeting publication standards.

---

**Rating: 5 (Marginally below the acceptance threshold)**

While the approach seems reasonable and the experimental results look promising, I have the following concerns about the paper. 

- The way they cite some of the existing works is not very precise, and thus I don't have a good impression of the quality of the work. For instance, [6] and [7] are the same book;  [26] on inverse distance weighting is Wikipedia; [32] and [33] are the same paper; [40] on Matern kernels is a webpage of scikit learn; [50] on radial basis functions is a webpage of scipy; [61] and [62] are the same paper; [64] and [65] are the same paper. 

- In lines 32-34, the authors say ``always exactly using the values on the queried points as the conditional events may mismatch the function’s local landscape by overemphasizing the approximation accuracy of the global landscape.'' Similar arguments are made in lines 43-48. I feel confused to see this argument. In GP regression, we usually assume noisy outputs, and the use of a regularized matrix inversion (where the regularizer is given by the noise variance) incorporates this assumption by automatically smoothing the outputs. 

- In line 57, the authors say ``CobBO uses the Automatic Relevance Determination (ARD) Matérn 5/2 kernel.'' But it is not clear how the authors specify the weights of this ARD kernel, as this is also not described in the supplementary. And the authors do not explain the value of the length scale parameter in the kernel. 

- In line 12 of Algorithm 1, there exists an RBF interpolation, denoted by $R$. The authors should describe the explicit from this interpolator. While the authors seem to explain that the RBF interpolation and GP regression are different, they are essentially the same approach. As such, RBF interpolation requires the computational complexity cubic to the data size. This point should also be discussed.

- In line 14 of Algorithm 1, $\Omega_t$ denotes the subspace spanned by the block coordinates $C_t$. I am wondering about how the authors specify the values for the coordinates in $\Omega_t$ not selected in $C_t$. For instance, suppose the dimensions are $D = 4$ and $C_t = \{1, 2\}$. Then is $x \in \Omega_t$ given in the form $x = (x_1, x_2, 0, 0)$ by specifying the coordinates not in $C_t$ (i.e,. \{3, 4\}) to be zeros? This point is not clear from the presentation. If this is the case, the authors implicitly make an assumption about the optimal solution that some of their coordinates are zero. This is rather a strong assumption.

- In Section 3.3, the authors define ``regret'' in terms of the selection of the best coordinate block. This regret is different from the regrets defined in terms of the global optimizer, usually analyzed in the Bayesian optimization literature. Therefore, the theoretical results of the paper do not really provide a theoretical guarantee for the proposed approach in terms of the convergence to the global optimizer.  

- While the authors show the hyperparameter settings in Table 2 of the supplementary, some information is still missing, such as the acquisition function used and the length scales of the kernels in RBF interpolation and GP regression. 


Clarity:
- Since the authors omit the concrete expressions for many mathematical concepts (e.g., RBF interpolation and the subspace $\Omega_t$), it is not very easy to deeply understand the mechanism of the proposed algorithm. Also, some key information for reproducing the experimental results is missing. 

Significance:
- There are many hyperparameters that need to be chosen in the proposed algorithm. (Table 2 in the supplementary shows an incomplete list of the hyperparameters.) If the performance of the proposed algorithm is sensitive to the choice of these parameters, the algorithm is not easy to be used by practitioners. This point is not clear from the paper. If this is the case, the significance of the proposed method is limited. 

- Also, as described above, it may be the case that the proposed approach implicitly assumes that some of the coordinates of the global optimizer are zeros. If this is the case, the proposed approach can only be applied to problems that satisfy this assumption, which may be restrictive.


------------ Post rebuttal --------------
As the authors made some clarifications, I have increased my score to 5. Still, however, I'm not very convinced by the argument of the authors on regret analysis.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
The authors do not discuss the limitations of their work.

Rating:
5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a new High-dim BO framework inspired by Block coordinate ascent. Although similar, this paper differs from other works which leverages a low dimensional structure assumption in that it utilizes all the dimension in the function space. Aside from the overall scheme, its other contributions include (1) an update scheme for the coordinate sampling distribution; (2) a heuristic stopping rule to balance between exploiting current coordinate block and exploring new coordinate blocks; and (3) a theoretical analysis that upper bounds the total regret on a surrogate loss function. The framework shows some promising results when compared with other high-dim BO works.
reviews.main_review:
--Clarity
The paper is generally well written and easy to follow. I have some minor confusions:

1. In the algorithm, the posterior is described as p(\hat{f}| \hat{H}). Is the posterior specified by a GP with low dimensional kernel or high dimensional kernel, but the length-scales on inactive coordinates are frozen (not learned)?.

2. At the beginning of section 4.2, it was claimed that CobBO was compared to REMBO and ATPE but I don't see the comparison anywhere. Also, what would  BOHB be in Fig.5 ?

--Originality
The motivation behind doing BO along a subset of coordinates has been explored many times in the literature. This paper argues that the proposed method, CobBO, "efficiently leverages all the observations in the whole space ... rather than only relying on limited observations in each coordinate subspace". As far as I understand, DROPOUT [35] also re-selects the active coordinates at every iteration (and fill in the remaining coordinates using some heuristic strategy), which guarantees making use of all coordinates in the long run. However, I believe that the proposed subspace selection algorithm is more informed due to the update scheme of the sampling distribution.
--Quality
My main concern with this paper is that the theoretical analysis is not conventional (with respect to the BO literature). In my experience, BO regret analysis usually measures the regret on the objective function itself rather than a surrogate loss. It could be useful to assert that the sampling scheme will, in the asymptotic sense, always pick the right subspace to improve the objective the most number of times; but it does not inform us by how much does it improve the objective. It seems to me that the proposed regret bound is an analysis of the coordinate descent aspect alone, and is entirely independent of the optimization routine within each selected subspace (i.e., if I replace BO by something else, would anything change?). 

Please refer to the Significance subsection for my comments on the experiments.

--Significance
1. For some experiments (e.g., Levy 200D and Ackley 200D), I don't think all methods have converged by the end of 10000 iterations. It would probably take longer to conclude that one is clearly superior to another. 
2. I would suggest some ablation study to show the effect of having variable coordinate block size |C_t| (as I understand, it is now capped at 30). On average, how many coordinates are sampled per iterations? Is there a lower bound to prevent the scheme from selecting none? How does it affect performance if we increase/decrease the upper bound?
3. It was claimed that REMBO takes too long for Ackley 200D, but I dont see how it is the case if we limit the number of effective dimension (to be roughly the same as the average no. selected coordinates by CobBO)? Seems to me that both REMBO and CobBO optimize a low dimensional GP at every iteration with the same no. observations, so what makes one significantly slower than the other?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: The authors have described the Broader impact of this paper, which I find reasonable. I believe there would be no potential negative societal impact.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

reviews.text:
# Peer Review - Rewritten

## Summary

The paper introduces a novel High-dimensional Bayesian Optimization framework grounded in Block Coordinate Ascent principles. While this approach shares conceptual similarities with existing methods, it distinguishes itself by engaging all dimensions of the function space rather than relying solely on low-dimensional structural assumptions. Key contributions include: (1) a mechanism for adaptively updating the coordinate sampling distribution; (2) a heuristic termination criterion designed to trade off between deepening exploitation within the current coordinate block and broadening exploration across new blocks; and (3) theoretical results establishing an upper bound on cumulative regret with respect to a surrogate loss function. Empirical findings demonstrate competitive or superior performance relative to existing high-dimensional BO approaches.

## Main Review

**Clarity Issues**

The manuscript is generally clear and accessible. However, several points warrant clarification:

1. The algorithmic description of the posterior p(\hat{f}| \hat{H}) requires additional specification. Is this posterior derived from a GP employing a low-dimensional kernel, or does it use a high-dimensional kernel with frozen length-scales on inactive dimensions?

2. Section 4.2 references comparative analysis with REMBO and ATPE, yet these comparisons do not appear in the presented results. Additionally, the identity and role of BOHB in Figure 5 remains unclear.

**Originality Assessment**

Coordinate-subset-based BO has received substantial prior attention in the literature. The authors contend that CobBO "makes efficient use of all observations across the entire space ... as opposed to relying exclusively on sparse observations within individual coordinate subspaces." However, DROPOUT [35] similarly re-samples active coordinates at each iteration (with remaining coordinates filled via heuristic methods), thereby ensuring comprehensive utilization of all coordinates over time. The primary novelty appears to reside in the coordinate selection mechanism itself, which benefits from a more principled updating scheme for the sampling distribution compared to alternatives.

**Quality Concerns**

The most significant issue is the unconventional nature of the theoretical analysis relative to standard BO literature. Conventional BO regret bounds typically measure regret against the objective function directly, rather than a surrogate loss. While it would be valuable to establish that the coordinate selection scheme asymptotically prioritizes subspaces offering maximum objective improvement, the current analysis does not quantify the magnitude of such improvement. The proposed regret bound appears to analyze only the coordinate descent mechanics in isolation, independent of the optimization procedure executed within each selected subspace—suggesting that swapping BO for an alternative optimization method would leave the analysis unaffected.

See the Significance section below for additional experimental comments.

**Significance and Experimental Concerns**

1. Several benchmark problems (e.g., Levy 200D and Ackley 200D) appear not to have reached convergence within the 10,000 iteration budget. Extended evaluation horizons would be necessary to definitively establish performance superiority.

2. An ablation study examining the impact of variable coordinate block size |C_t| (currently capped at 30) would strengthen the work. Specifically: What is the average number of sampled coordinates per iteration? Does the algorithm enforce a minimum threshold to prevent empty selections? How sensitive is performance to modifications of the upper bound?

3. The claim that REMBO becomes computationally prohibitive on Ackley 200D requires justification. If REMBO's effective dimensionality is constrained to match the average number of coordinates selected by CobBO, both methods would optimize a low-dimensional GP at each iteration using comparable data. The computational disparity between the two approaches thus remains unexplained.

**Limitations and Societal Impact**

The authors have provided a reasonable discussion of broader impacts. No significant societal risks are apparent from this work.

## Rating

**5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold**

The paper presents interesting ideas and demonstrates empirical promise, but the theoretical analysis lacks the rigor and relevance expected for high-dimensional BO, key experimental comparisons are missing or inadequately explained, and important ablation studies are absent. With revisions addressing these concerns—particularly strengthening the theoretical contributions and clarifying experimental comparisons—this work could reach acceptance quality.

title: Distributional Generalization: Characterizing Classifiers Beyond Test Error

reviews.summary: The paper empirically characterizes a fine-grained generalization property of interpolating learning algorithms, and give a conjecture that formalizes the observed phenomenon. Roughly, the paper observes that the generalization properties of an interpolating classifier can be quite distinct for different sample subpopulations with the same label, whenever the label noise imposed on these subpopulations during the training phase is distinct. Authors conjecture that the generalization properties may be uniform only for samples belonging to a smaller "distinguishable" clusters of the input data space.
reviews.main_review:
Post-rebuttal: I thank authors for their detailed response. Most of the authors' response made a perfect sense. One thing that prevented me from raising the score further is that I am still not sure about is whether the conjecture itself is meaningful or useful. In fact, after reading the response to my first point (Converse...), I am slightly more pessimistic about the possibility that "distinguishability" and "feature calibration" are tightly connected concepts (say, in a causal sense). I am curious if there are alternate (and perhaps more useful) conjectures that can help explain the phenomenon. In other words, I think the paper should benefit from having an extensive discussion that justifies why the proposed conjecture based on distinguishability is nontrivial, useful, and better than other possible alternatives.


---

__What I like.__ The reported empirical observation is intriguing to some degree, and (in my opinion) is worthy of documenting and formalizing. Authors validate the observation under a variety of scenarios in Section 1 & 4 (and also appendices), which helped convincing me of the existence of such phenomenon. As the authors noted, establishing a theoretical framework that can explain the depicted phenomenon may be an interesting open problem (perhaps we can phrase problems in a minimax style with multiple tasks?). The clarity of the writing is well above average, while there are several points that can be clarified further. I also liked the theoretical contributions, which could help readers grasp the idea of how such conjectures can be proved.

__Checking/refining the conjecture.__ On the one hand, I find that the provided empirical observations support the conjecture well. On the other hand, I think that one can even further strengthen the argument by trying to provide an evidence supporting that the conditions described in the conjecture is indeed essential for the distributional similarity. I am actually very curious whether the "distinguishable features" are really essential. Is there a case where the distributional similarity does not hold for indistinguishable features, which is perhaps more informative than the perfectly random case? Although this is not really necessary if one is interested only in the validity of the conjecture, having such evidence will help us understand how non-vacuous/"tight" the conjecture is.

__Clarifying the terminology: Interpolating.__ I think the term "interpolating classifier" can be clarified further in the text. Although the terminology is now (sort of) popularly used in the literature, I think still they need to be properly defined at least once in the main text, as the modern use of this terminology are often wider than what is used to mean in the classic kernel-based literature.

__Clarifying the terminology: Distributional Closeness.__ I am curious in what sense the notion of "distributional closeness" introduced in Section 2 is different from the widely used notion of integral probability metrics (IPMs). If they are identical, sticking to the existing notation would help reduce the confusion.

__Practical implication.__ While I mostly liked the theoretical implications illustrated in Section 1.3, I wonder if authors could give an idea why capturing such fine-grained generalization properties may be important. For instance, could there be any implications to the fairness or shortcut learning?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: The limitation is clearly described, in my opinion. This work mainly discusses what is implicitly taking place in the standard learning practices, instead of giving a new one. I do not think there is any "potential negative impact" that should be discussed.

reviews.text:
# Peer Review (Rewritten)

## Summary

This work provides an empirical investigation into a nuanced generalization phenomenon exhibited by interpolating learning algorithms, accompanied by a formal conjecture capturing this behavior. The key finding is that interpolating classifiers demonstrate differentiated generalization performance across distinct sample subpopulations sharing the same label when those subpopulations experience varying levels of label noise during training. The authors propose that generalization uniformity may only hold within smaller "distinguishable" clusters in the input space.

## Main Review

**Post-rebuttal comments:** I appreciate the authors' thorough and thoughtful responses to my initial concerns. The majority of their arguments are compelling and well-reasoned. However, my hesitation in increasing the score stems from lingering doubts regarding whether the proposed conjecture itself constitutes a meaningful and practically useful contribution. Following the authors' clarification on my initial concern about converse implications, I have become somewhat more skeptical about the degree to which "distinguishability" and "feature calibration" are fundamentally linked (whether in a mechanistic or causal manner). I would welcome seeing the authors explore whether alternative conjectures might provide more insightful or actionable explanations of the observed phenomenon. Consequently, the paper would benefit substantially from an in-depth treatment that demonstrates the non-triviality, practical utility, and superiority of the proposed distinguishability-based conjecture relative to competing explanatory frameworks.

## Strengths

The empirical observation itself is genuinely compelling and merits formal documentation and investigation. The validation across multiple experimental settings in Sections 1 and 4, as well as the appendices, effectively demonstrates the robustness of the phenomenon. The authors appropriately identify that developing a rigorous theoretical framework to account for this behavior represents an important open question, potentially amenable to formulations drawing on multi-task minimax perspectives. The exposition demonstrates strong clarity, though some aspects would benefit from additional precision. The theoretical contributions are valuable in illuminating how such conjectures might be formally established.

## Detailed Comments

**On conjecture validation:** While the empirical evidence reasonably supports the proposed conjecture, the argument could be substantially strengthened by demonstrating that the conditions articulated in the conjecture are genuinely necessary for achieving distributional similarity. Specifically, I am interested in whether "distinguishable features" truly play an essential role. Would it be possible to identify scenarios where distributional similarity fails under indistinguishable features—cases more informative than purely random baselines? Such evidence would provide insight into the conjecture's tightness and substantive content, though I acknowledge this may exceed the scope if the focus is solely on conjecture validity.

**On terminology—interpolating classifiers:** The term "interpolating classifier" warrants more explicit definition within the main text. While this terminology has gained currency in contemporary literature, definitions vary across communities and differ from classical kernel-based usage. A precise definition early in the paper would enhance accessibility and prevent ambiguity.

**On terminology—distributional closeness:** I would appreciate clarification regarding how the "distributional closeness" notion introduced in Section 2 relates to the established framework of integral probability metrics (IPMs). Should these concepts coincide, adopting standard IPM notation would minimize potential confusion and improve coherence with existing literature.

**On practical significance:** While I found the theoretical implications discussed in Section 1.3 largely persuasive, the paper would be strengthened by articulating why understanding such fine-grained generalization properties matters in practice. Could the authors elaborate on potential applications to domains such as algorithmic fairness or the mitigation of shortcut learning? Such connections would underscore the broader relevance of the work.

## Limitations and Societal Impact

The limitations are articulated with appropriate clarity. The work characterizes implicit phenomena in standard learning settings rather than proposing novel methodologies. I see no significant societal risks requiring discussion.

## Recommendation

**Rating: 6 (Marginally above the acceptance threshold)**

The paper makes a solid empirical and conceptual contribution by documenting and formalizing an interesting generalization phenomenon. However, uncertainties regarding the conjecture's meaningfulness and utility prevent a stronger recommendation. With revisions addressing the conjecture's necessity, comparative advantages over alternatives, and practical implications, this work could make a more compelling contribution.

For instance, could there be any implications to the fairness or shortcut learning?

Limitations And Societal Impact:
The limitation is clearly described, in my opinion. This work mainly discusses what is implicitly taking place in the standard learning practices, instead of giving a new one. I do not think there is any "potential negative impact" that should be discussed.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary:
This submission proposes a new type of generalization satisfied by interpolating classifiers (the primary focus), that is different from agreement between test error and train error as in classical learning theory. This type of generalization is largely motivated by empirical evidence across different models (neural networks, kernel regression & svm, decision trees) and theoretically for 1-Nearest Neighbors classification (Theorem 1). More specifically, the submission claims that for certain types of mappings x -> L(x) (termed "distinguishable features" -- i.e. they must be learnable to good test accuracy in the setting under consideration), the joint distribution of (L(x), y) where (x,y) pairs are drawn from the true distribution will be "close" to the joint distribution of (L(x), f(x)) on the test set, where f(x) is the trained model prediction.

List of contributions:
--Proposes that interpolating classifiers satisfy "distributional generalization." This is first more specifically formulated as the "Feature Calibration Conjecture" (FCC) in Section 3. A crucial component of this is to identify the types of features L(x) for which the calibration holds (Definition 1); roughly, if the feature is learnable from inputs x to good test accuracy in the setting under consideration (fixed algorithm, data distribution, number of samples, allowable error), then under that same setting calibration will be achieved. The authors prove FCC is true for 1-Nearest neighbors. The Feature Calibration Conjecture is not fully specified, in that there is an assumption of "natural distribution" D (which is such that the conjecture holds in the empirical settings considered).

--Empirical test of the FCC. The types of distinguishable features L considered include (i) the constant partition (calibration then implies the marginal distribution p(y) of the predicted labels matches that of the true label distribution); (ii) a "coarse partition" (L(x) is an "easy" classification task) and calibration holds for fine-grained label subgroups, for instance in Imagenet; (iii) "class partition" (classes that can be learned well from standard datasets, and the true label distribution is a noisy corruption of this); (iv) multiple features. Models investigated include neural networks (WideResnets and MLPs), kernels (standard and high-performing ones), and decision trees. 

--A definition for "distributional generalization" which subsumes the definition of feature calibration & classical generalization, and very preliminary evidence that this holds for non-interpolating models (a Wide Resnet model on CIFAR-10 during training).
reviews.main_review:
Originality: This work identifies & investigates an original empirical property that appears to be satisfied by interpolating classifiers. The proposed concepts (Feature Calibration) are general & seem distinct from any prior work to the best of my knowledge. (For example, the authors note that "standard" calibration is a particular case of Feature Calibration with specific choice of L(x).) I think this work is bold in what it seeks to accomplish (asking a fundamentally new question at the basis of learning theory, building evidence from diverse empirical examples) and makes a reasonable first step towards doing so. 

Quality: The quality of the work seems high. The authors give a number of different empirical examples with sufficient coverage across model diversity and provide a theoretically satisfied case (1-Nearest Neighbors). The authors note the limitations of the work (e.g. the FCC is specified in terms of "natural distributions" which has an unclear meaning) and are fairly thorough in addressing potential concerns & connections to other works. I was not able to identify significant shortcomings with respect to the conceptual formulation or message of the paper. I make some comments about diversity of empirical examples (with respect to datasets / partitions) below.

Clarity: The paper is clearly written and was straightforward to read.

Significance: I think this paper takes a risk in its premise, provides a reasonable amount of evidence in support of its main claims, and would spur further work in this area; as I was also not able to identify any major issues with the paper, this is the reason behind my suggested score. In Section 1.3 the authors discuss a number of consequences for learning theory which seem notable. This work tells us more about the implicit bias of models (though not just neural networks); most interesting I think is that in certain limits (e.g. when models are interpolating, and model size >> dataset size), models are not approaching the Bayes optimal classifier but sample from the distribution p(y|x). 

Other comments & questions:

--I find some mentions of "Bayes optimal classifier" to be confusing at times -- for instance, its usage in L268 seems to be with respect to argmax p(y | L(x)) (rather than argmax p(y|x)), if I understand correctly. Perhaps it is worth pointing this out and the connection between the two (e.g. in the setting where p(y|x) is discussed, section starting at L107). 

--In the empirical examples with label noise, why do the authors consider only sparse confusion matrices?

--I think this work has good empirical diversity across very distinct classes of interpolating models, but one place where the paper could be noticeably strengthened is diversity of examples with respect to the datasets / partitions L(x) that are considered. Apart from the constant partition L(x) = 0, all the partitions seem to be largely based off of semantic distinctions that are already available in datasets. (Because of this, I also wondered whether the behavior is largely inherited from the dataset and from the existing semantic structure within it.) Are there more complex L(x), that are not semantically constructed, that can be considered? Separate but related to this, is it valid to consider L(x) that have some knowledge of the distribution D, or even a particular training set? For instance, L(x) that is constructed from trained intermediate layer embeddings in a neural network -- or would L(x) that has knowledge of the distribution D potentially break FCC?

(For instance, this type of L(x) / y constructed from true labels / label noise on existing label structure, e.g. CIFAR-10, is also used in the non-interpolating setting in Section 5.2, which makes the reader wonder what is actually happening in the interpolating setting. More generally, I found Section 5.2 to be in tension with the main message prior to that section, and perhaps too preliminary to be presentable.)

--I think one other experiment that would significantly strengthen the paper is one that tests the implications mentioned for scaling limits in L107 (e.g. measure feature calibration as models are scaled from the underparametered scaling regime to overparameterized.) This would give some more diverse evidence of places where feature calibration breaks down.

Nit points:
--Typo L263: spare -> "sparse"
--Typo L265: Figure label should be "2A"
--L749 in appendix is cut off
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact:
The paper does not address potential negative societal impact, but I believe it is not necessary given the nature of the work.

The authors do provide adequate discussion of limitations and are clear about the conjectural portions of their work.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

reviews.text:
# Peer Review - Rewritten

## Summary

The paper introduces a novel form of generalization applicable to interpolating classifiers that diverges from the traditional notion of convergence between training and test error found in classical learning theory. The work draws primary motivation from extensive empirical observations across diverse model classes (neural networks, kernel methods, SVMs, and decision trees) and provides theoretical validation for the 1-Nearest Neighbors case (Theorem 1). The central claim posits that for certain feature mappings x -> L(x) (termed "distinguishable features" -- characterized by their learnability to high test accuracy in the given setting), the joint distribution P(L(x), y) under the true data distribution will closely match the empirical joint distribution P(L(x), f(x)) on test samples, where f denotes the learned model.

## Contributions

--Introduces the concept of "distributional generalization" for interpolating classifiers, formalized through the "Feature Calibration Conjecture" (FCC) in Section 3. A key element involves characterizing which feature mappings L(x) exhibit calibration (Definition 1); specifically, features that can be learned from inputs to high test accuracy under given conditions (fixed algorithm, data distribution, sample size, error tolerance) will satisfy calibration under those same conditions. The authors establish FCC for 1-Nearest neighbors and prove it theoretically. However, the FCC remains incompletely specified, as it relies on an assumption regarding "natural distributions" D (distributions under which the conjecture holds in the empirical settings examined).

--Empirical validation of the FCC across multiple feature types: (i) the trivial partition (calibration implies predicted label marginal matches true label marginal); (ii) "coarse partitions" (L(x) represents an "easy" classification task) with calibration verified on fine-grained label subgroups in datasets like ImageNet; (iii) "class partitions" (readily learnable classes from standard datasets where true labels are noisy corruptions thereof); (iv) combinations of multiple features. Experiments span neural networks (WideResnets and MLPs), kernel methods (standard and state-of-the-art variants), and decision trees.

--A formal definition of "distributional generalization" that encompasses both feature calibration and classical generalization notions, accompanied by preliminary evidence suggesting this property extends to non-interpolating models (WideResnet on CIFAR-10 during training).

## Main Review

**Originality**: The work identifies and examines an original empirical phenomenon exhibited by interpolating classifiers. The proposed concepts, particularly Feature Calibration, appear novel and distinct from existing literature to my knowledge. (The authors correctly note that standard calibration represents a special case of Feature Calibration with particular choices of L(x).) The paper demonstrates ambition in pursuing a foundational question for learning theory and assembles compelling preliminary evidence from varied empirical settings.

**Quality**: The work demonstrates strong quality throughout. The authors present numerous empirical demonstrations with adequate breadth across different model classes and provide theoretical validation through the 1-Nearest Neighbors case. The authors transparently acknowledge limitations (notably, the unclear specification of "natural distributions" in FCC) and thoroughly address potential concerns and connections to related work. I found no significant conceptual or presentational flaws in the work's formulation or central message. Some observations regarding the scope of empirical examples appear below.

**Clarity**: The paper is well-written and accessible. The exposition flows logically and presents complex ideas in an understandable manner.

**Significance**: The paper takes a principled risk by proposing a novel premise, marshals reasonable supporting evidence, and would likely catalyze future research directions. Combined with the absence of major technical issues, this assessment supports the proposed rating. Section 1.3 articulates several implications for learning theory that merit attention. The work advances our understanding of implicit bias across model classes (not limited to neural networks); particularly noteworthy is the finding that under certain conditions (interpolating models, model size >> dataset size), models do not converge to the Bayes optimal classifier but instead sample from p(y|x).

## Additional Comments and Questions

--Certain discussions of the "Bayes optimal classifier" create ambiguity -- for instance, the usage at L268 appears to reference argmax p(y | L(x)) rather than argmax p(y|x), if I interpret correctly. Clarifying this distinction and explicitly connecting these formulations (particularly in the section beginning at L107 where p(y|x) is discussed) would strengthen the presentation.

--In experiments involving label noise, what motivates the restriction to sparse confusion matrices? This choice warrants explanation.

--The paper demonstrates commendable empirical breadth across fundamentally different interpolating model classes, yet could be substantially improved by expanding diversity in datasets and partition choices L(x). Excluding the trivial constant partition, nearly all examined partitions derive from semantic structures already inherent in the datasets. (This raises the question of whether observed behavior primarily reflects dataset properties and existing semantic organization.) Would the analysis benefit from incorporating more sophisticated L(x) constructions lacking semantic grounding? Additionally, can L(x) leverage knowledge of distribution D or specific training sets, or would such constructions violate FCC? (For instance, L(x) derived from trained neural network embeddings -- or would L(x) incorporating distributional knowledge potentially compromise FCC?)

(The reliance on label noise and semantic structure in Section 5.2, mirroring the interpolating setting examples, creates some tension with the paper's main narrative, and the preliminary nature of Section 5.2 raises questions about its readiness for publication.)

--The paper would gain substantial strength from empirical investigation of the scaling implications mentioned at L107 -- specifically, measuring feature calibration as models transition from underparameterized to overparameterized regimes. Such experiments would provide additional evidence characterizing where feature calibration fails.

## Minor Issues

--Typo at L263: "spare" should read "sparse"
--Typo at L265: Figure reference should be "2A"
--Text at L749 in appendix appears incomplete

## Limitations and Societal Impact

The paper does not discuss potential negative societal implications, though given the theoretical nature of the work, such discussion is not essential.

The authors adequately discuss limitations and clearly delineate which portions represent conjectures versus established results.

## Rating

**7: Good paper, accept**

This work makes a meaningful contribution to fundamental learning theory by identifying and characterizing an important empirical phenomenon. Despite some areas for enhancement, the originality, quality, and potential significance of the contribution warrant acceptance.

reviews.summary: The authors consider interpolating classifiers, i.e. classifiers that interpolate the training data by selecting the correct class for training samples, often performing poorly in terms of generalization error. The authors propose a new notion of generalization, i.e. the Distributional Generalization. Such a notion does not focus on the average error over the inputs, and subsumes both classical generalization and the conjecture that the outputs of classifiers match the statistics of their training distribution when conditioned on certain subgroups. The authors formally introduce and experimentally validate a conjecture to specify such subgroups. While the conjecture is proven for 1-nearest-neighbor classifiers, empirical evidence is provided for other classifiers; such as kernel machines and neural networks.
reviews.main_review:
== Main comments ==

- The paper is overall well written and the topics are clearly exposed. Illustrative examples are effectively presented to further clarify the problem at hand.
- The presented work tackles a timely and relevant problem in machine learning, drawing ideas from many related areas of research, such as output distribution calibration, overparametrization, and benign overfitting. The presented results are worthy but not completely surprising. 
- The paper addresses a very much similar problem than : 
Vapnik, V. & Izmailov, R.. (2020). Complete statistical theory of learning: learning using statistical invariants. Proceedings of the Ninth Symposium on Conformal and Probabilistic Prediction and Applications, in PMLR 128:4-40
However, this problem is known to below to the class of  ill-posed problems. How does the current work related to the above mentioned reference? How does it overcome the ill-posed problem. 
- In eq. (1) the notation is a bit confusing. Are TrainSet and TestSet the distributions from which the samples of these sets are drawn (but then D_tr, D_te are used to mean the same), or rather the sets themselves (and then ~ should be replaced with ∈)?
- One of the two main contributions is thoroughly expressed in section 3.2. This is indeed a very interesting observation. However it looks closely related to the problem defined in section 2 of [26].
Could the authors further underline the value and originality of their contribution by discussing the connection between the two concepts?
- Although the feature calibration conjecture is fully proved to 1-nearest-neighbors classifiers, it is also true that it represents a very limited model. It seems that for more complex (and more commonly used) models, only empirical evidence can be provided so far, with no much theoretical formalization. Although this is not necessarily a negative aspect for works in progress and contributions which bring new ideas, the expectation set up by the writers in the introductory section are diminished by lack of formalization.
- The training procedure is sometimes indicated as Train_A, and sometimes as A. We suggest to choose one symbol and consistently use it throughout the whole paper.
- Have the authors thought of any potential issue related to identifying potentially sensitive subgroups L such as, ethnicity, high/low income, etc. instead of dogs, cats, etc. when using different
datasets?
- We would like to draw attention on the fact that, in line 101, the reference to the work in [7] does not seem to properly fit it. Indeed, in [7] the authors already explain that ‘benign overfitting’ is only characterized in specific cases and does not generally apply, making the claim in lines 103-104 appear a bit excessive. Reference [26] should be reported as the version officially published at ICML 2017.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: The authors take into the scientific limitations of their work in section 3.4. Further improvements can be implemented by answering the questions and doubts raised in the Main Review.
reviews.rating: 4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.
